# 2 U.S. Code § 1604 - Reports by registered lobbyists

- [U.S. Code](#tab_default_1)
- [Notes](#tab_default_2)


[prev](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603 " Registration of lobbyists") | [next](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1605 " Disclosure and enforcement")

(a) Quarterly report

No later than 20 days after the end of the quarterly period beginning on the first day of January, April, July, and October of each year in which a registrant is registered under [section 1603 of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603), or on the first business day after such 20th day if the 20th day is not a business day, each registrant shall file a report with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives on its[lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)during such quarterly period. A separate report shall be filed for each[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)of the registrant.

(b) Contents of reportEach quarterly report filed under subsection (a) shall contain—

(1)

the name of the registrant, the name of the [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), and any changes or updates to the information provided in the initial registration, including information under [section 1603(b)(3) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#b_3);

(2) for each general issue area in which the registrant engaged in [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on behalf of the[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)during the quarterly period—

(A)

a list of the specific issues upon which a [lobbyist](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) employed by the registrant engaged in [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), including, to the maximum extent practicable, a list of bill numbers and references to specific executive branch actions;

(B)

a statement of the Houses of Congress and the Federal agencies contacted by[lobbyists](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)employed by the registrant on behalf of the[client;](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)

(C)

a list of the [employees](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) of the registrant who acted as [lobbyists](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on behalf of the[client;](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) and

(D)

a description of the interest, if any, of any [foreign entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1616049327-1414555995&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) identified under [section 1603(b)(4) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#b_4) in the specific issues listed under subparagraph (A);

(3)

in the case of a [lobbying firm](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1790013038-1414555998&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), a good faith estimate of the total amount of all income from the[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)(including any payments to the registrant by any other person for [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on behalf of the[client)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) during the quarterly period, other than income for matters that are unrelated to [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604);

(4)

in the case of a registrant engaged in [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on its own behalf, a good faith estimate of the total expenses that the registrant and its[employees](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)incurred in connection with [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) during the quarterly period;

(5)

for each [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), immediately after listing the [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), an identification of whether the [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) is a[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)or local government or a department,[agency,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) special purpose district, or other instrumentality controlled by one or more[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)or local governments; and

(6)

for any listed [lobbyist](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) who was convicted in a Federal or[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)court of an offense involving bribery, extortion, embezzlement, an illegal kickback, tax evasion, fraud, a conflict of interest, making a false statement, perjury, or money laundering, the date of the conviction and a description of the offense.

(c) Estimates of income or expensesFor purposes of this section, estimates of income or expenses shall be made as follows:

(1)

Estimates of amounts in excess of $5,000 shall be rounded to the nearest $10,000.

(2)

In the event income or expenses do not exceed $5,000, the registrant shall include a statement that income or expenses totaled less than $5,000 for the reporting period.

(d) Semiannual reports on certain contributions

(1) In generalNot later than 30 days after the end of the semiannual period beginning on the first day of January and July of each year, or on the first business day after such 30th day if the 30th day is not a business day, each person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) who is registered or is required to register under paragraph (1) or (2) of [section 1603(a) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#a), and each[employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)who is or is required to be listed as a[lobbyist](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)under [section 1603(b)(6) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#b_6) or subsection (b)(2)(C) of this section, shall file a report with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives containing—

(A)

the name of the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604);

(B)

in the case of an [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), his or her employer;

(C)

the names of all political committees established or controlled by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604);

(D)

the name of each Federal candidate or officeholder, [leadership PAC](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1083094889-2114428541&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), or political party committee, to whom aggregate contributions equal to or exceeding $200 were made by the person or[organization,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or a political committee established or controlled by the person or[organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)within the semiannual period, and the date and amount of each such contribution made within the semiannual period;

(E) the date, recipient, and amount of funds contributed or disbursed during the semiannual period by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or a political committee established or controlled by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)—

(i)

to pay the cost of an event to honor or recognize a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or[covered executive branch official;](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)

(ii)

to an entity that is named for a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), or to a[person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)in recognition of such official;

(iii)

to an entity established, financed, maintained, or controlled by a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or[covered executive branch official,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or an entity designated by such official; or

(iv)

to pay the costs of a meeting, retreat, conference, or other similar event held by, or in the name of, 1 or more [covered legislative branch officials](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or[covered executive branch officials,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)

except that this subparagraph shall not apply if the funds are provided to a person who is required to report the receipt of the funds under [section 30104 of title 52](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/52/30104);

(F)

the name of each Presidential library foundation, and each Presidential inaugural committee, to whom contributions equal to or exceeding $200 were made by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), or a political committee established or controlled by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), within the semiannual period, and the date and amount of each such contribution within the semiannual period; and

(G) a certification by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) filing the report that the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)—

(i)

has read and is familiar with those provisions of the Standing Rules of the Senate and the Rules of the House of Representatives relating to the provision of gifts and travel; and

(ii)

has not provided, requested, or directed a gift, including travel, to a Member of Congress or an officer or[employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)of either House of Congress with knowledge that receipt of the gift would violate rule XXXV of the Standing Rules of the Senate or rule XXV of the Rules of the House of Representatives.

(2) Definition

In this subsection, the term “[leadership PAC](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1083094889-2114428541&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)” has the meaning given such term in [section 30104(i)(8)(B) of title 52](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/52/30104#i_8_B).

(e) Electronic filing required

A report required to be filed under this section shall be filed in electronic form, in addition to any other form that the Secretary of the Senate or the Clerk of the House of Representatives may require or allow. The Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives shall use the same electronic software for receipt and recording of filings under this chapter.

([Pub. L. 104–65, § 5](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._104-65), Dec. 19, 1995, [109 Stat. 697](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/109_Stat._697); [Pub. L. 105–166, § 4(c)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._105-166), Apr. 6, 1998, [112 Stat. 39](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/112_Stat._39); [Pub. L. 110–81, title II](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), §§ 201(a), (b)(6), 202, 203(a), 205, 207(a)(2), Sept. 14, 2007, [121 Stat. 741](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/121_Stat._741), 742, 746, 747; [Pub. L. 115–418, § 2(b)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._115-418), Jan. 3, 2019, [132 Stat. 5440](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/132_Stat._5440).)

**Editorial Notes**

References in Text

This chapter, referred to in subsec. (e), was in the original “this Act” meaning [Pub. L. 104–65](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._104-65), Dec. 19, 1995, [109 Stat. 691](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/109_Stat._691), known as the [Lobbying Disclosure Act of 1995](https://www.law.cornell.edu/topn/lobbying_disclosure_act_of_1995). For complete classification of this Act to the Code, see Short Title note set out under [section 1601 of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1601) and Tables.

Amendments

2019—Subsec. (b)(6). [Pub. L. 115–418](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._115-418) added par. (6).

2007—Subsec. (a). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 201(a)(1)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), substituted, in heading, “Quarterly” for “Semiannual” and, in text, “20 days after the end of the quarterly period beginning on the first day of January, April, July, and October of each year in which a registrant is registered under [section 1603 of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603), or on the first business day after such 20th day if the 20th day is not a business day,” for “45 days after the end of the semiannual period beginning on the first day of each January and the first day of July of each year in which a registrant is registered under [section 1603 of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603),” and “such quarterly period” for “such semiannual period”.

Subsec. (b). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 201(a)(2)(A)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), substituted “quarterly report” for “semiannual report” in introductory provisions.

Subsec. (b)(1). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 207(a)(2)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), inserted “, including information under [section 1603(b)(3) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#b_3)” before semicolon.

Subsec. (b)(2). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 201(a)(2)(B)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), substituted “quarterly period” for “semiannual filing period” in introductory provisions.

Subsec. (b)(3). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 201(a)(2)(C)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), substituted “quarterly period” for “semiannual period”.

Subsec. (b)(4). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 201(a)(2)(D)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), substituted “quarterly period” for “semiannual filing period”.

Subsec. (b)(5). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 202](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), added par. (5).

Subsec. (c)(1). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 201(b)(6)(A)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), substituted “$5,000” for “$10,000” and “$10,000” for “$20,000”.

Subsec. (c)(2). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 201(b)(6)(B)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), substituted “$5,000” for “$10,000” in two places.

Subsec. (d). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 203(a)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), added subsec. (d).

(3) which read as follows: “A registrant that reports lobbying expenditures pursuant to [section 6033(b)(8) of title 26](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/26/6033#b_8) may satisfy the requirement to report income or expenses by filing with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives a copy of the form filed in accordance with section 6033(b)(8).”

# 2 U.S. Code § 1604 - Reports by registered lobbyists

- [U.S. Code](#tab_default_1)
- [Notes](#tab_default_2)


[prev](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603 " Registration of lobbyists") | [next](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1605 " Disclosure and enforcement")

(a) Quarterly report

No later than 20 days after the end of the quarterly period beginning on the first day of January, April, July, and October of each year in which a registrant is registered under [section 1603 of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603), or on the first business day after such 20th day if the 20th day is not a business day, each registrant shall file a report with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives on its[lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)during such quarterly period. A separate report shall be filed for each[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)of the registrant.

(b) Contents of reportEach quarterly report filed under subsection (a) shall contain—

(1)

the name of the registrant, the name of the [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), and any changes or updates to the information provided in the initial registration, including information under [section 1603(b)(3) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#b_3);

(2) for each general issue area in which the registrant engaged in [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on behalf of the[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)during the quarterly period—

(A)

a list of the specific issues upon which a [lobbyist](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) employed by the registrant engaged in [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), including, to the maximum extent practicable, a list of bill numbers and references to specific executive branch actions;

(B)

a statement of the Houses of Congress and the Federal agencies contacted by[lobbyists](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)employed by the registrant on behalf of the[client;](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)

(C)

a list of the [employees](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) of the registrant who acted as [lobbyists](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on behalf of the[client;](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) and

(D)

a description of the interest, if any, of any [foreign entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1616049327-1414555995&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) identified under [section 1603(b)(4) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#b_4) in the specific issues listed under subparagraph (A);

(3)

in the case of a [lobbying firm](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1790013038-1414555998&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), a good faith estimate of the total amount of all income from the[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)(including any payments to the registrant by any other person for [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on behalf of the[client)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) during the quarterly period, other than income for matters that are unrelated to [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604);

(4)

in the case of a registrant engaged in [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) on its own behalf, a good faith estimate of the total expenses that the registrant and its[employees](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)incurred in connection with [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) during the quarterly period;

(5)

for each [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), immediately after listing the [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), an identification of whether the [client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) is a[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)or local government or a department,[agency,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) special purpose district, or other instrumentality controlled by one or more[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)or local governments; and

(6)

for any listed [lobbyist](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) who was convicted in a Federal or[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)court of an offense involving bribery, extortion, embezzlement, an illegal kickback, tax evasion, fraud, a conflict of interest, making a false statement, perjury, or money laundering, the date of the conviction and a description of the offense.

(c) Estimates of income or expensesFor purposes of this section, estimates of income or expenses shall be made as follows:

(1)

Estimates of amounts in excess of $5,000 shall be rounded to the nearest $10,000.

(2)

In the event income or expenses do not exceed $5,000, the registrant shall include a statement that income or expenses totaled less than $5,000 for the reporting period.

(d) Semiannual reports on certain contributions

(1) In generalNot later than 30 days after the end of the semiannual period beginning on the first day of January and July of each year, or on the first business day after such 30th day if the 30th day is not a business day, each person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) who is registered or is required to register under paragraph (1) or (2) of [section 1603(a) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#a), and each[employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)who is or is required to be listed as a[lobbyist](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)under [section 1603(b)(6) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603#b_6) or subsection (b)(2)(C) of this section, shall file a report with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives containing—

(A)

the name of the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604);

(B)

in the case of an [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), his or her employer;

(C)

the names of all political committees established or controlled by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604);

(D)

the name of each Federal candidate or officeholder, [leadership PAC](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1083094889-2114428541&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), or political party committee, to whom aggregate contributions equal to or exceeding $200 were made by the person or[organization,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or a political committee established or controlled by the person or[organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)within the semiannual period, and the date and amount of each such contribution made within the semiannual period;

(E) the date, recipient, and amount of funds contributed or disbursed during the semiannual period by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or a political committee established or controlled by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)—

(i)

to pay the cost of an event to honor or recognize a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or[covered executive branch official;](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)

(ii)

to an entity that is named for a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), or to a[person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)in recognition of such official;

(iii)

to an entity established, financed, maintained, or controlled by a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or[covered executive branch official,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or an entity designated by such official; or

(iv)

to pay the costs of a meeting, retreat, conference, or other similar event held by, or in the name of, 1 or more [covered legislative branch officials](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) or[covered executive branch officials,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)

except that this subparagraph shall not apply if the funds are provided to a person who is required to report the receipt of the funds under [section 30104 of title 52](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/52/30104);

(F)

the name of each Presidential library foundation, and each Presidential inaugural committee, to whom contributions equal to or exceeding $200 were made by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), or a political committee established or controlled by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604), within the semiannual period, and the date and amount of each such contribution within the semiannual period; and

(G) a certification by the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604) filing the report that the person or [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)—

(i)

has read and is familiar with those provisions of the Standing Rules of the Senate and the Rules of the House of Representatives relating to the provision of gifts and travel; and

(ii)

has not provided, requested, or directed a gift, including travel, to a Member of Congress or an officer or[employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)of either House of Congress with knowledge that receipt of the gift would violate rule XXXV of the Standing Rules of the Senate or rule XXV of the Rules of the House of Representatives.

(2) Definition

In this subsection, the term “[leadership PAC](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1083094889-2114428541&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1604)” has the meaning given such term in [section 30104(i)(8)(B) of title 52](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/52/30104#i_8_B).

(e) Electronic filing required

A report required to be filed under this section shall be filed in electronic form, in addition to any other form that the Secretary of the Senate or the Clerk of the House of Representatives may require or allow. The Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives shall use the same electronic software for receipt and recording of filings under this chapter.

([Pub. L. 104–65, § 5](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._104-65), Dec. 19, 1995, [109 Stat. 697](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/109_Stat._697); [Pub. L. 105–166, § 4(c)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._105-166), Apr. 6, 1998, [112 Stat. 39](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/112_Stat._39); [Pub. Subsec. (b)(5). [Pub. L. 110–81, § 202](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._110-81), added par. (5).

(3) which read as follows: “A registrant that reports lobbying expenditures pursuant to [section 6033(b)(8) of title 26](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/26/6033#b_8) may satisfy the requirement to report income or expenses by filing with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives a copy of the form filed in accordance with section 6033(b)(8).”

lastPrompt: Hi. I've got a set of US Congressional lobbying filings and press releases in this workspace, and there's a file called data_manual.md that explains how it's all laid out. Please treat my files as rea…

content.text: I now have the actual statutory text. This is the decisive legal point: 2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E) — added by HLOGA in 2007 — *requires disclosure* of exactly these payments (events honoring an official, entities named for an official, entities the official controls) but imposes **no dollar cap**, unlike capped FECA campaign contributions. So the channel is legal by design; the story is the channel and its concentration, not illegality. Let me record the case file and write up findings. First, the existing thread format:

input.command: cd /sessions/wizardly-fervent-mayer/mnt/CorpusData/investigation_lead1/casefile && echo "===THREAD SAMPLE===" && cat threads/*.md | head -40 && echo "===ENTITIES SAMPLE===" && head -30 entities.yaml && echo "===FINDINGS SAMPLE===" && head -40 findings.yaml

content.text:
===THREAD SAMPLE===
---
id: T-0001
title: "No gatekeeper — fabricated $80M 'sovereign nation' in the official LDA record"
status: confirmed          # open | chasing | confirmed | cold | killed
priority: 1
workflow_step: "mapper anomaly scan; raw-filing review; multi-year recompute; checking-the-law; GAO audit"
entities: [state-of-loc-nation, loc-community-association, us-chamber-of-commerce, usao-dc, sec-senate-clerk-house]
findings: [F-001, F-002, F-003, F-004]
source_records:
  - "data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json#4d5b1cb0-0971-43b8-958b-d260e2be8af1"
  - "data/senate/2024/filings/filings_2024.json#5000632b-e40b-4762-affc-551b46a40c91"
  - "house/2025_1stQuarter_XML/301694892.xml"
  - "GAO-25-107523 (2024 Lobbying Disclosure audit)"
next_action: >
  Reporting-out only (finding is confirmed from records): seek comment from every named party incl.
  the filer; interview Secretary of the Senate Office of Public Records / House Clerk on content review;
  obtain DOJ 2 U.S.C. 1605(b) semiannual enforcement reports for the authoritative all-time count;
  lawyer review of 18 U.S.C. 1001(c) reach and of any wrongdoing implication.
---

## Hypothesis (confirmed)
The federal lobbying-disclosure system has no gatekeeper: it accepts and publishes self-certified
filings with no content check on the way in, and the government enforces the law almost entirely
against non-filers on the way out. A single fabricated filing proves both ends.

## What's documented (see findings F-001..F-004)
- A fictitious 'sovereign nation' reported $80M in 2025 lobbying fees (F-001).
- That figure is ~21x the next real client by income and exceeds the largest real in-house budget,
  US Chamber ~$70.3M (F-002).
- It distorted national totals (~1.3% of all reported lobbying dollars; 2.78% of income only) (F-002).
- The 'no dollar figure' gap is structural (~10-12% of activity reports every year) (F-003).
- A false filing is illegal in principle (2 U.S.C. 1606; maybe 18 U.S.C. 1001) but effectively
  unenforced; GAO shows enforcement aimed at non-filers, no documented false-content penalty (F-004).

## Framing rule (non-negotiable)
Story is about the SYSTEM, not the filer. Every legal hook needs intent to deceive; a sincere-belief
filer may be outside the statutes entirely — which is itself why the spotlight belongs on the absent
gatekeeper, not the individual. Assert only "possible, for a lawyer." No party contacted yet.

## Corrections applied in fact-check pass (2026-07-15)
===ENTITIES SAMPLE===
# Entity registry. Each entry: canonical_id, display, type/status, why.
members: []

firms:
  - canonical_id: loc-community-association
    display: "Loc Community Association (registrant)"
    type: registrant
    status: key
    why: >
      Senate registrant_id 401108853 / house_registrant_id 56638. Self-registered to lobby for its
      own "client" (State of Loc Nation). Contact: /Christina Loren Clement LLC/, 8 The Green Suite A,
      Dover DE 19901. The vehicle through which the $80M fabricated figure entered the record.

  - canonical_id: usao-dc
    display: "U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Columbia"
    type: enforcer
    status: context
    why: >
      Sole LDA enforcement referral destination. Per GAO-25-107523, handles thousands of non-filing
      referrals; one $65k civil settlement in 2024; zero criminal; no false-content case documented.

  - canonical_id: sec-senate-clerk-house
    display: "Secretary of the Senate / Clerk of the House (LDA filing offices)"
    type: administrator
    status: context
    why: >
      Operate the LDA e-filing portals. 2 U.S.C. 1605 tasks them to review/verify accuracy, but in
      practice publish self-certified filings immediately with no substantive content check. The
      'no gatekeeper on the way in'.

===FINDINGS SAMPLE===
# Findings ledger. Each finding needs >=1 source_record.
# "documented" = present in / derived from public records. Does NOT assert causation or intent.
# All figures re-derived from raw data and fact-checked 2026-07-15 (corrected numbers below).

findings:
  - id: F-001
    thread: T-0001
    status: confirmed
    claim: >
      A single filer registered a fictitious entity — client "STATE OF LOC NATION GLOBAL PUBLIC
      BENEFIT CORPORATION" (Senate client_id 61287), self-described as an "Established sovereign govt"
      — via a self-registered registrant "Loc Community Association" (registrant_id 401108853), and
      reported $20,000,000 of lobbying income for each quarter of 2025 = $80,000,000 for the year

===THREAD SAMPLE===
---
id: T-0001
title: "No gatekeeper — fabricated $80M 'sovereign nation' in the official LDA record"
status: confirmed          # open | chasing | confirmed | cold | killed
priority: 1
workflow_step: "mapper anomaly scan; raw-filing review; multi-year recompute; checking-the-law; GAO audit"
entities: [state-of-loc-nation, loc-community-association, us-chamber-of-commerce, usao-dc, sec-senate-clerk-house]
findings: [F-001, F-002, F-003, F-004]
source_records:
  - "data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json#4d5b1cb0-0971-43b8-958b-d260e2be8af1"
  - "data/senate/2024/filings/filings_2024.json#5000632b-e40b-4762-affc-551b46a40c91"
  - "house/2025_1stQuarter_XML/301694892.xml"
  - "GAO-25-107523 (2024 Lobbying Disclosure audit)"
next_action: >
  Reporting-out only (finding is confirmed from records): seek comment from every named party incl.
  the filer; interview Secretary of the Senate Office of Public Records / House Clerk on content review;
  obtain DOJ 2 U.S.C. 1605(b) semiannual enforcement reports for the authoritative all-time count;
  lawyer review of 18 U.S.C. 1001(c) reach and of any wrongdoing implication.
---

## Hypothesis (confirmed)
The federal lobbying-disclosure system has no gatekeeper: it accepts and publishes self-certified
filings with no content check on the way in, and the government enforces the law almost entirely
against non-filers on the way out. A single fabricated filing proves both ends.

## What's documented (see findings F-001..F-004)
- A fictitious 'sovereign nation' reported $80M in 2025 lobbying fees (F-001).
- That figure is ~21x the next real client by income and exceeds the largest real in-house budget,
  US Chamber ~$70.3M (F-002).
- It distorted national totals (~1.3% of all reported lobbying dollars; 2.78% of income only) (F-002).
- The 'no dollar figure' gap is structural (~10-12% of activity reports every year) (F-003).
- A false filing is illegal in principle (2 U.S.C. 1606; maybe 18 U.S.C. 1001) but effectively
  unenforced; GAO shows enforcement aimed at non-filers, no documented false-content penalty (F-004).

## Framing rule (non-negotiable)
Story is about the SYSTEM, not the filer. Every legal hook needs intent to deceive; a sincere-belief
filer may be outside the statutes entirely — which is itself why the spotlight belongs on the absent
gatekeeper, not the individual. Assert only "possible, for a lawyer." No party contacted yet.

## Corrections applied in fact-check pass (2026-07-15)

input.content:
---
id: T-0004
title: "The 'in honor of' channel — uncapped corporate giving to member-tied charities/buildings/events (LD-203)"
status: confirmed          # open | chasing | confirmed | cold | killed
priority: 1
workflow_step: "extract non-FECA contribution_items (type=he) 2022-2026Q1; cluster by honoree/payee/registrant; web-verify top items; checking-the-law on 2 USC 1604(d)"
entities: [honorary-giving-channel, cbcf, chci, rpk-mcconnell-building, statefarm-foxx-habitat]
findings: [F-005, F-006, F-007]
source_records:
  - "data/senate/2022..2026/contributions/contributions_*.json (contribution_type='he', 4,291 items)"
  - "2 U.S.C. 1604(d)(1)(E) (read at law.cornell.edu, as-of 2026-07-15)"
next_action: >
  Reporting-out only (pattern confirmed from records + outside verification). Seek comment from
  State Farm, Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft; the McConnell office / Republican Party of Kentucky; the
  Congressional Black Caucus Foundation and Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute; and Rep. Foxx.
  Optional deepening: pull IRS 990s for CBCF/CHCI to see the corporate-donor share; check whether the
  Comcast/MetLife/Altria RPK gifts reported in KY press appear anywhere in the federal LD-203 set
  (they do not in the Senate data — a possible disclosure-completeness angle).
---

## Hypothesis (confirmed as a documented pattern)
Beyond capped FECA campaign contributions, lobbying registrants disclose a second, far less-scrutinized
money channel on their semiannual LD-203 reports: "Honorary Expenses" — money given to charities,
foundations, buildings, archives, and honoring events tied to specific members of Congress. This
channel is uncapped by statute, flows from the biggest corporate lobbying interests, and concentrates
around (a) the caucus-affiliated nonprofits and (b) a handful of individual power-center members.

## What's documented (see F-005..F-007)
- Scale: 4,291 honorary items, 2022-2026 Q1, totaling ~$134.4M reported; steady ~$30-38M/year (F-005).
- The giver is the registrant (contributor field is almost always "SELF"). Top registrant-givers are
  Amazon $7.16M, State Farm $5.94M, Meta $5.40M, AARP $3.86M, Comcast $3.44M, Toyota, Morgan Stanley,
  Chevron, Google (F-005).
- Biggest destinations cluster in caucus nonprofits: Congressional Black Caucus Foundation ~$25.5M
  from 101 distinct corporate funders; Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute + Leadership Institute
  ~$22.7M from 80 funders; APAICS ~$5.0M from 39 funders (F-005).
- Individual power-center concentration: the McConnell cluster (F-006) and the single largest item of
  all — State Farm $2.65M to Habitat for Humanity "in honor of" Rep. Virginia Foxx (F-007).

## Legal status (checking-the-law, 2 U.S.C. 1604(d))
NOT a violation. HLOGA (Pub. L. 110-81 sec. 203, 2007) added 1604(d)(1)(E), which *requires*
disclosure of exactly these payments — (i) costs of an event honoring a covered official; (ii) money
to an entity named for, or given in recognition of, a covered official; (iii) money to an entity the
official established/finances/maintains/controls; (iv) costs of an event held in a covered official's
name — but sets NO dollar cap and only a $200 threshold appears elsewhere (political contributions,
subpar. D). The exception routes anything FEC-reportable to the FECA bucket instead, so these items
are by construction the *non-campaign* money. The filer certifies it did not give a gift that would
break House/Senate gift rules. So the channel is legal-by-design and disclosed; the story is the
channel + concentration, not illegality. A quid-pro-quo/gift-rule/gratuity question (18 U.S.C. 201,
1346; chamber gift rules) would only arise if facts showed personal benefit or a linked official act —
NOT provable from these filings, and lawyers'-territory only.

## Framing rules (non-negotiable)
- Describe as "reported/disclosed," and as money to the charity/entity, NOT to the member personally.
- Do not imply bribery or illegality; the strongest legal statement is "legal but under-scrutinized."
- Seek comment from every named party before publication; lawyer review before any influence framing.

---
id: T-0005
title: "Corporate lobbyists funding the 'Mitch McConnell Building' and McConnell-named institutions"
status: confirmed
priority: 2
workflow_step: "he-cluster on honoree/payee ~ McConnell; separate U-of-Louisville self-report from corporate money; web-verify Pfizer $1M/RPK"
entities: [rpk-mcconnell-building, honorary-giving-channel]
findings: [F-006]
source_records:
  - "data/senate/2022/contributions/contributions_2022.json#7fcdd899-5c8c-46cd-94c3-9fa9eb8993b5 (Pfizer $1M)"
  - "data/senate/2022/contributions/contributions_2022.json#19b89a8f-3cf2-4d2b-9d8b-5cff6e59163d (AT&T $100k)"
  - "data/senate/2023/contributions/contributions_2023.json#4419ba3f-37df-4ee0-a2df-103180b7a117 (Microsoft $100k)"
  - "https://kentuckylantern.com/2023/01/09/pfizer-gives-1-million-to-republican-party-of-kentucky-to-expand-its-headquarters/"
next_action: >
  Reporting-out. Seek comment from Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft, Delta; Republican Party of Kentucky;
  McConnell's office. Note the disclosure-completeness angle: KY press reported Comcast $100k, MetLife
  $300k, Altria $100k to the same building fund, but those do NOT appear in the Senate LD-203 'he'
  data — worth confirming whether they filed elsewhere or under a different type.
---

## Hypothesis (confirmed)
Corporate lobbying interests routed honorary money to institutions named for, or in recognition of,
Senate Republican Leader Mitch McConnell — most vividly the Republican Party of Kentucky "Building
Fund," whose headquarters is the "Mitch McConnell Building."

## What's documented (F-006)
- Republican Party of Kentucky / McConnell-building cluster: 8 LD-203 'he' items, ~$1.525M, 4 funders:
  Pfizer $1,000,000 (2022-12-27), AT&T $100k x3 (2022-24), Microsoft $100k (2023) + $25k (2025),
  Delta $50k x2 (2022) — all with honoree = McConnell / RPK building.
- Broader "in honor of McConnell" corporate giving (excluding U of Louisville self-report): ~$1.81M
  across 28 items / 18 funders, also reaching think-tank galas (AEI, Hudson) and the JFK Center.
- Separately, University of Louisville self-reports ~$2.53M to the "McConnell Center" and
  "McConnell-Chao Archives" — that is the university disclosing its own endowment/archive spending on
  a named center, NOT corporate influence money; keep it distinct.

## Outside verification
Pfizer's $1M to the Republican Party of Kentucky Building Fund is independently confirmed by Kentucky
Lantern, WKMS, and Hoptown Chronicle (Jan 2023); the HQ is named the "Mitch McConnell Building"; press
also names Comcast, MetLife, AT&T, Altria, Delta as fund donors. The filings match reality.

## Legal status
Textbook 2 U.S.C. 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii) disclosures (money to an entity named for / in recognition of a
covered official). Legal and disclosed; no cap. Not a violation. See T-0004 legal note.

---
id: T-0006
title: "Largest single honorary item: State Farm $2.65M to Habitat for Humanity 'in honor of' Rep. Virginia Foxx"
status: confirmed
priority: 2
workflow_step: "he max-item scan; web-verify against State Farm/Habitat public announcement"
entities: [statefarm-foxx-habitat, honorary-giving-channel]
findings: [F-007]
source_records:
  - "data/senate/2025/contributions/contributions_2025.json#91514e3d-2443-4e6e-943c-0b9bd47e4de0"
  - "https://www.habitat.org/newsroom/2025/state-farm-celebrates-more-30-years-partnership-habitat-humanity-international"
next_action: >
  Reporting-out. Ask State Farm and Rep. Foxx's office why a company-wide Habitat partnership renewal
  is reported on a lobbying disclosure "in honor of" a specific sitting House member, and what the
  connection is (was Foxx honored at a Habitat event?). Seek comment before publication.
---

## Hypothesis (confirmed as documented, with a genuine interpretive question)
The single largest honorary item in the entire 2022-2026Q1 corpus is State Farm Mutual Automobile
Insurance Co. reporting a $2,650,000 payment to Habitat for Humanity International, honoree
"Rep. Virginia Foxx," dated 2025-11-20 (uuid 91514e3d-...).

## Outside verification (this is why we don't trust the form alone)
Unlike the lead-1 fabricated filing, this money is REAL: State Farm publicly announced in June 2025 it
was renewing its 30-year Habitat partnership and increasing contributions to ~$2.6M for youth
engagement and resilient-housing programs (habitat.org newsroom, 2025). The dollar figure and the
State Farm->Habitat relationship check out.

## The reportable nuance
What is notable is not the gift's legality but the attribution: routine, company-wide corporate
philanthropy appears on a federal LOBBYING disclosure tagged "in honor of" one sitting House member
(Foxx chairs / has chaired Education & the Workforce and Rules). Either Foxx was formally honored in
connection with the gift (a 1604(d)(1)(E)(i)/(ii) event) or the honoree tag is a loose attribution.
Both readings are worth a question to State Farm and Foxx's office. No cap applies; not a violation.

  - "data/senate/2025/contributions/contributions_2025.json#91514e3d-2443-4e6e-943c-0b9bd47e4de0"
  - "https://www.habitat.org/newsroom/2025/state-farm-celebrates-more-30-years-partnership-habitat-humanity-international"
next_action: >
  Reporting-out. Ask State Farm and Rep. Foxx's office why a company-wide Habitat partnership renewal
  is reported on a lobbying disclosure "in honor of" a specific sitting House member, and what the
  connection is (was Foxx honored at a Habitat event?). Seek comment before publication.
---

## Hypothesis (confirmed as documented, with a genuine interpretive question)
The single largest honorary item in the entire 2022-2026Q1 corpus is State Farm Mutual Automobile
Insurance Co. reporting a $2,650,000 payment to Habitat for Humanity International, honoree
"Rep. Virginia Foxx," dated 2025-11-20 (uuid 91514e3d-...).

## Outside verification (this is why we don't trust the form alone)
Unlike the lead-1 fabricated filing, this money is REAL: State Farm publicly announced in June 2025 it
was renewing its 30-year Habitat partnership and increasing contributions to ~$2.6M for youth
engagement and resilient-housing programs (habitat.org newsroom, 2025). The dollar figure and the
State Farm->Habitat relationship check out.

## The reportable nuance
What is notable is not the gift's legality but the attribution: routine, company-wide corporate
philanthropy appears on a federal LOBBYING disclosure tagged "in honor of" one sitting House member
(Foxx chairs / has chaired Education & the Workforce and Rules). Either Foxx was formally honored in
connection with the gift (a 1604(d)(1)(E)(i)/(ii) event) or the honoree tag is a loose attribution.
Both readings are worth a question to State Farm and Foxx's office. No cap applies; not a violation.

next_action: >
  Reporting-out only (pattern confirmed from records + outside verification). Seek comment from
  State Farm, Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft; the McConnell office / Republican Party of Kentucky; the
  Congressional Black Caucus Foundation and Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute; and Rep. Foxx.
  Optional deepening: pull IRS 990s for CBCF/CHCI to see the corporate-donor share; check whether the
  Comcast/MetLife/Altria RPK gifts reported in KY press appear anywhere in the federal LD-203 set
  (they do not in the Senate data — a possible disclosure-completeness angle).
---

## Hypothesis (confirmed as a documented pattern)
Beyond capped FECA campaign contributions, lobbying registrants disclose a second, far less-scrutinized
money channel on their semiannual LD-203 reports: "Honorary Expenses" — money given to charities,
foundations, buildings, archives, and honoring events tied to specific members of Congress. This
channel is uncapped by statute, flows from the biggest corporate lobbying interests, and concentrates
around (a) the caucus-affiliated nonprofits and (b) a handful of individual power-center members.

## What's documented (see F-005..F-007)
- Scale: 4,291 honorary items, 2022-2026 Q1, totaling ~$134.4M reported; steady ~$30-38M/year (F-005).
- The giver is the registrant (contributor field is almost always "SELF"). Top registrant-givers are
  Amazon $7.16M, State Farm $5.94M, Meta $5.40M, AARP $3.86M, Comcast $3.44M, Toyota, Morgan Stanley,
  Chevron, Google (F-005).
- Biggest destinations cluster in caucus nonprofits: Congressional Black Caucus Foundation ~$25.5M
  from 101 distinct corporate funders; Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute + Leadership Institute
  ~$22.7M from 80 funders; APAICS ~$5.0M from 39 funders (F-005).
- Individual power-center concentration: the McConnell cluster (F-006) and the single largest item of
  all — State Farm $2.65M to Habitat for Humanity "in honor of" Rep. Virginia Foxx (F-007).

## Legal status (checking-the-law, 2 U.S.C. 1604(d))
NOT a violation. HLOGA (Pub. L. 110-81 sec. 203, 2007) added 1604(d)(1)(E), which *requires*
disclosure of exactly these payments — (i) costs of an event honoring a covered official; (ii) money
to an entity named for, or given in recognition of, a covered official; (iii) money to an entity the
official established/finances/maintains/controls; (iv) costs of an event held in a covered official's
name — but sets NO dollar cap and only a $200 threshold appears elsewhere (political contributions,
subpar. D). The exception routes anything FEC-reportable to the FECA bucket instead, so these items
are by construction the *non-campaign* money. The filer certifies it did not give a gift that would
break House/Senate gift rules. So the channel is legal-by-design and disclosed; the story is the
channel + concentration, not illegality. A quid-pro-quo/gift-rule/gratuity question (18 U.S.C. 201,
1346; chamber gift rules) would only arise if facts showed personal benefit or a linked official act —
NOT provable from these filings, and lawyers'-territory only.

## Framing rules (non-negotiable)
- Describe as "reported/disclosed," and as money to the charity/entity, NOT to the member personally.
- Do not imply bribery or illegality; the strongest legal statement is "legal but under-scrutinized."
- Seek comment from every named party before publication; lawyer review before any influence framing.

content.text: Numbers reproduce. Now the findings memo:

input.content:
# The "in honor of" money channel — findings memo

**Investigation:** Beyond capped campaign donations, companies give money "in honor of" politicians —
to charities, foundations, buildings, archives, and honoring events tied to them. Who gives, how much,
where does it go, and does anything cluster or stand out?
**Data:** Senate LD-203 semiannual contribution reports, 2022 – 2026 Q1 (`data/senate/*/contributions/`).
**Status:** Pattern confirmed from records; top items cross-checked against outside sources; legality
checked against the actual statute. Ready for reporting-out (comment + lawyer review) before any use.
**A framing note up front:** this money is *disclosed and legal*. It goes to charities and entities,
not to members personally. Nothing here asserts anyone broke a law. The story is the *channel* and
*where it concentrates* — a lightly-watched lane that sits beside the capped campaign-money lane.

---

## What this channel is

Every lobbying registrant must file a semiannual "LD-203" report. Alongside its capped political (FECA)
contributions, it must itemize a second kind of money: **"Honorary Expenses"** — payments made to honor
or recognize a specific member of Congress or executive-branch official. In the data this is the
`contribution_type = "he"` line item, with three fields that tell the story: **who paid** (the filer),
**the payee** (where the money went — a charity, foundation, building fund, or event vendor), and the
**honoree** (the politician it was "in honor of").

This is the channel Congress deliberately made visible in 2007 (see the legal section). It is the money
that is *not* a campaign contribution — and, unlike campaign contributions, **it is not capped.**

## The shape of it — who, how much, where

Across 2022 through 2026 Q1 there are **4,291 honorary items totaling ~$134.4 million reported**, running
a steady ~$30–38M a year. A few structural facts:

- **The giver is the company, not an individual.** On 93% of items the "contributor" field just says
  "SELF" — the real giver is the filing registrant. The biggest corporate givers by this measure are
  Amazon ($7.16M), State Farm ($5.94M), Meta ($5.40M), AARP ($3.86M), Comcast ($3.44M), Toyota ($3.43M),
  Morgan Stanley ($3.40M), Chevron ($3.09M), and Google ($2.91M).
- **Where it goes is highly concentrated.** The single largest destination bucket is the
  **caucus-affiliated nonprofits**:

  | Recipient cluster | Reported (he) | Distinct corporate funders |
  |---|---|---|
  | Congressional Black Caucus Foundation | ~$25.5M | 101 |
  | Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute + Leadership Institute | ~$22.7M | 80 |
  | Asian Pacific American Institute for Congressional Studies (APAICS) | ~$5.0M | 39 |

  These are the galas and scholarship foundations tied to the caucuses; corporate lobbying interests
  fund them "in honor of" caucus members. This is the backbone of the whole channel by dollar volume.

*(A related, non-campaign layer exists but is a separate, smaller-N story: Presidential Inaugural
Committee contributions ~$67.6M across 120 items, and Presidential Library contributions ~$22.4M across
120. Those are worth their own look but aren't "in honor of a member of Congress.")*

## The two standouts worth a closer look

### 1. Corporate lobbyists funding the "Mitch McConnell Building"

Setting aside the University of Louisville (which self-reports ~$2.5M of its own spending on the named
"McConnell Center" and "McConnell-Chao Archives" — that's the university, not influence money), the
sharp finding is corporate money flowing to the **Republican Party of Kentucky "Building Fund," whose
headquarters is literally named the "Mitch McConnell Building."** Eight honorary items, ~$1.525M, four
funders:

- **Pfizer — $1,000,000** (Dec 27, 2022)
- **AT&T — $100,000 × 3** (2022–2024)
- **Microsoft — $100,000** (2023) plus **$25,000** (2025)
- **Delta — $50,000 × 2** (2022)

**Verified outside the filings.** Kentucky Lantern, WKMS, and the Hoptown Chronicle all reported Pfizer's
$1M gift to the party building fund in January 2023, confirmed the HQ is named for McConnell, and named
additional corporate donors (Comcast, MetLife, Altria) to the same fund. So the filings match reality
here — and interestingly, some of those press-named donors (Comcast, MetLife, Altria) do **not** appear
in the federal LD-203 "he" data, which is itself a disclosure-completeness question worth chasing.
Broader "in honor of McConnell" corporate giving (think-tank galas at AEI and Hudson, the JFK Center)
adds up to ~$1.81M across 28 items / 18 funders.

### 2. The single largest honorary item — State Farm's $2.65M "in honor of" Rep. Virginia Foxx

The biggest honorary item in the entire four-year corpus: **State Farm reporting $2,650,000 to Habitat
for Humanity International, honoree "Rep. Virginia Foxx,"** dated Nov 20, 2025.

**This is exactly why we don't trust the form at face value.** Given the prior investigation's fabricated
$80M filing, a $2.65M line demands a check. It checks out: State Farm publicly announced in June 2025 it
was renewing its 30-year Habitat partnership and raising contributions to ~$2.6M for youth and
resilient-housing programs. The money and the relationship are real. **The reportable nuance is the
attribution** — routine, company-wide corporate philanthropy shows up on a federal *lobbying* disclosure
"in honor of" one specific sitting House member. Either Foxx was formally honored in connection with the
gift, or the honoree tag is loose. That's a question for State Farm and Foxx's office, not a violation.

## Is any line crossed? (the law, actually read)

**No clear legal line — and that's the point.** The governing provision is **2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E)**
(read at law.cornell.edu, as-of 2026-07-15), added by the Honest Leadership and Open Government Act of
2007 (HLOGA, Pub. L. 110-81 § 203). It *requires* filers to disclose payments made:

- (i) to pay the cost of an **event honoring** a covered official;
- (ii) to an **entity named for**, or given **in recognition of**, a covered official;
- (iii) to an **entity the official establishes, finances, maintains, or controls**;
- (iv) to pay for a meeting/retreat/**event held in the official's name**.

Two things follow directly from the text. First, this is a **disclosure requirement, not a prohibition**
— Congress built this channel to be visible, and the filings above are doing exactly what the law asks.
Second, and central to your question: **there is no dollar cap.** Capped FECA campaign contributions are
disclosed elsewhere (and the statute's only threshold, $200, applies to those political contributions);
subparagraph (E) sets *no ceiling at all* and even carves out anything FEC-reportable, so these items are
by design the *uncapped, non-campaign* money. A company can give a member-named building fund $1M and
simply report it.

A bribery/gratuity or gift-rule question (chamber gift rules; 18 U.S.C. §§ 201, 1346) would only arise if
facts showed the money **personally benefited** a member or was tied to a **specific official act** — and
that cannot be shown from these filings. It is lawyers'-and-prosecutors' territory, not something to
assert. **Verdict: legal but under-scrutinized. Not a violation.**

## Before any of this is used

Seek comment from every named party — State Farm and Rep. Foxx; Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft, Delta, the
Republican Party of Kentucky, and Sen. McConnell's office; and the Congressional Black Caucus Foundation
and Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute. Have a lawyer review before publishing any influence
implication. No party has been contacted.

# The "in honor of" money channel — findings memo

**Investigation:** Beyond capped campaign donations, companies give money "in honor of" politicians —
to charities, foundations, buildings, archives, and honoring events tied to them. Who gives, how much,
where does it go, and does anything cluster or stand out?
**Data:** Senate LD-203 semiannual contribution reports, 2022 – 2026 Q1 (`data/senate/*/contributions/`).
**Status:** Pattern confirmed from records; top items cross-checked against outside sources; legality
checked against the actual statute. Ready for reporting-out (comment + lawyer review) before any use.
**A framing note up front:** this money is *disclosed and legal*. It goes to charities and entities,
not to members personally. Nothing here asserts anyone broke a law. The story is the *channel* and
*where it concentrates* — a lightly-watched lane that sits beside the capped campaign-money lane.

---

## What this channel is

Every lobbying registrant must file a semiannual "LD-203" report. Alongside its capped political (FECA)
contributions, it must itemize a second kind of money: **"Honorary Expenses"** — payments made to honor
or recognize a specific member of Congress or executive-branch official. In the data this is the
`contribution_type = "he"` line item, with three fields that tell the story: **who paid** (the filer),
**the payee** (where the money went — a charity, foundation, building fund, or event vendor), and the
**honoree** (the politician it was "in honor of").

This is the channel Congress deliberately made visible in 2007 (see the legal section). It is the money
that is *not* a campaign contribution — and, unlike campaign contributions, **it is not capped.**

## The shape of it — who, how much, where

Across 2022 through 2026 Q1 there are **4,291 honorary items totaling ~$134.4 million reported**, running
a steady ~$30–38M a year. A few structural facts:

- **The giver is the company, not an individual.** On 93% of items the "contributor" field just says
  "SELF" — the real giver is the filing registrant. The biggest corporate givers by this measure are
  Amazon ($7.16M), State Farm ($5.94M), Meta ($5.40M), AARP ($3.86M), Comcast ($3.44M), Toyota ($3.43M),
  Morgan Stanley ($3.40M), Chevron ($3.09M), and Google ($2.91M).
- **Where it goes is highly concentrated.** The single largest destination bucket is the
  **caucus-affiliated nonprofits**:

  | Recipient cluster | Reported (he) | Distinct corporate funders |
  |---|---|---|
  | Congressional Black Caucus Foundation | ~$25.5M | 101 |
  | Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute + Leadership Institute | ~$22.7M | 80 |
  | Asian Pacific American Institute for Congressional Studies (APAICS) | ~$5.0M | 39 |

  These are the galas and scholarship foundations tied to the caucuses; corporate lobbying interests
  fund them "in honor of" caucus members. This is the backbone of the whole channel by dollar volume.

*(A related, non-campaign layer exists but is a separate, smaller-N story: Presidential Inaugural
Committee contributions ~$67.6M across 120 items, and Presidential Library contributions ~$22.4M across
120. Those are worth their own look but aren't "in honor of a member of Congress.")*

## The two standouts worth a closer look

### 1. Corporate lobbyists funding the "Mitch McConnell Building"

Setting aside the University of Louisville (which self-reports ~$2.5M of its own spending on the named
"McConnell Center" and "McConnell-Chao Archives" — that's the university, not influence money), the
sharp finding is corporate money flowing to the **Republican Party of Kentucky "Building Fund," whose
headquarters is literally named the "Mitch McConnell Building."** Eight honorary items, ~$1.525M, four
funders:

- **Pfizer — $1,000,000** (Dec 27, 2022)
- **AT&T — $100,000 × 3** (2022–2024)
- **Microsoft — $100,000** (2023) plus **$25,000** (2025)
- **Delta — $50,000 × 2** (2022)

**Verified outside the filings.** Kentucky Lantern, WKMS, and the Hoptown Chronicle all reported Pfizer's
$1M gift to the party building fund in January 2023, confirmed the HQ is named for McConnell, and named
additional corporate donors (Comcast, MetLife, Altria) to the same fund. So the filings match reality
here — and interestingly, some of those press-named donors (Comcast, MetLife, Altria) do **not** appear
in the federal LD-203 "he" data, which is itself a disclosure-completeness question worth chasing.
Broader "in honor of McConnell" corporate giving (think-tank galas at AEI and Hudson, the JFK Center)
adds up to ~$1.81M across 28 items / 18 funders.

### 2. The single largest honorary item — State Farm's $2.65M "in honor of" Rep. Virginia Foxx

The biggest honorary item in the entire four-year corpus: **State Farm reporting $2,650,000 to Habitat
for Humanity International, honoree "Rep. Virginia Foxx,"** dated Nov 20, 2025.

**This is exactly why we don't trust the form at face value.** Given the prior investigation's fabricated
$80M filing, a $2.65M line demands a check. It checks out: State Farm publicly announced in June 2025 it
was renewing its 30-year Habitat partnership and raising contributions to ~$2.6M for youth and
resilient-housing programs. The money and the relationship are real. **The reportable nuance is the
attribution** — routine, company-wide corporate philanthropy shows up on a federal *lobbying* disclosure
"in honor of" one specific sitting House member. Either Foxx was formally honored in connection with the
gift, or the honoree tag is loose. That's a question for State Farm and Foxx's office, not a violation.

## Is any line crossed? (the law, actually read)

**No clear legal line — and that's the point.** The governing provision is **2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E)**
(read at law.cornell.edu, as-of 2026-07-15), added by the Honest Leadership and Open Government Act of
2007 (HLOGA, Pub. L. 110-81 § 203). It *requires* filers to disclose payments made:

- (i) to pay the cost of an **event honoring** a covered official;
- (ii) to an **entity named for**, or given **in recognition of**, a covered official;
- (iii) to an **entity the official establishes, finances, maintains, or controls**;
- (iv) to pay for a meeting/retreat/**event held in the official's name**.

Two things follow directly from the text. First, this is a **disclosure requirement, not a prohibition**
— Congress built this channel to be visible, and the filings above are doing exactly what the law asks.
Second, and central to your question: **there is no dollar cap.** Capped FECA campaign contributions are
disclosed elsewhere (and the statute's only threshold, $200, applies to those political contributions);
subparagraph (E) sets *no ceiling at all* and even carves out anything FEC-reportable, so these items are
by design the *uncapped, non-campaign* money. A company can give a member-named building fund $1M and
simply report it.

A bribery/gratuity or gift-rule question (chamber gift rules; 18 U.S.C. §§ 201, 1346) would only arise if
facts showed the money **personally benefited** a member or was tied to a **specific official act** — and
that cannot be shown from these filings. It is lawyers'-and-prosecutors' territory, not something to
assert. **Verdict: legal but under-scrutinized. Not a violation.**

## Before any of this is used

Seek comment from every named party — State Farm and Rep. Foxx; Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft, Delta, the
Republican Party of Kentucky, and Sen. McConnell's office; and the Congressional Black Caucus Foundation
and Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute. Have a lawyer review before publishing any influence
implication. No party has been contacted.

- Channel totals and clusters: `data/senate/2022..2026/contributions/contributions_*.json`,
  `contribution_type = "he"` — reproduced by `investigation_honorary/work/extract.py`, `profile.py`,
  `standouts.py`, `records.py`.
- Pfizer $1M item: `contributions_2022.json` filing_uuid `7fcdd899-5c8c-46cd-94c3-9fa9eb8993b5`.
- State Farm / Foxx item: `contributions_2025.json` filing_uuid `91514e3d-2443-4e6e-943c-0b9bd47e4de0`.
- Statute: 2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E), https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1604 (as-of 2026-07-15).
- Outside verification: Kentucky Lantern (Pfizer/RPK, Jan 2023); habitat.org newsroom (State Farm
