# 2 U.S. Code § 1602 - Definitions

- [U.S. Code](#tab_default_1)
- [Notes](#tab_default_2)


[prev](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1601 " Findings") | [next](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1603 " Registration of lobbyists")

As used in this chapter:

(1) Agency

The term “[agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” has the meaning given that term in [section 551(1) of title 5](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/5/551#1).

(2) Client

The term “[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means any [person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) that employs or retains another person for financial or other compensation to conduct [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) on behalf of that[person or entity.](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) A[person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)whose[employees](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)act as[lobbyists](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)on its own behalf is both a[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)and an employer of such[employees.](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) In the case of a coalition or association that employs or retains other persons to conduct [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), the[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)is the coalition or association and not its individual members.

(3) Covered executive branch officialThe term “[covered executive branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means—

(A)

the President;

(B)

the Vice President;

(C)

any officer or [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), or any other individual functioning in the capacity of such an officer or [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), in the Executive Office of the President;

(D)

any officer or [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) serving in a position in level I, II, III, IV, or V of the Executive Schedule, as designated by statute or Executive order;

(E)

any member of the uniformed services whose pay grade is at or above O–7 under [section 201 of title 37](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/37/201); and

(F)

any officer or [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) serving in a position of a confidential, policy-determining, policy-making, or policy-advocating character described in [section 7511(b)(2)(B) of title 5](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/5/7511#b_2_B).

(4) Covered legislative branch officialThe term “[covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means—

(A)

a Member of Congress;

(B)

an elected officer of either House of Congress;

(C) any [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) of, or any other individual functioning in the capacity of an [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) of—

(i)

a Member of Congress;

(ii)

a committee of either House of Congress;

(iii)

the leadership staff of the House of Representatives or the leadership staff of the Senate;

(iv)

a joint committee of Congress; and

(v)

a working group or caucus organized to provide legislative services or other assistance to Members of Congress; and

(D)

any other legislative branch [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) serving in a position described under [section 13101(13) of title 5](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/5/13101#13).

(5) EmployeeThe term “[employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means any individual who is an officer, [employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), partner, director, or proprietor of a [person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), but does not include—

(A)

independent contractors; or

(B)

volunteers who receive no financial or other compensation from the [person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) for their services.

(6) Foreign entity

The term “[foreign entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1616049327-1414555995&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means a foreign principal (as defined in section 1(b) of the [Foreign Agents Registration Act of 1938](https://www.law.cornell.edu/topn/foreign_agents_registration_act_of_1938) ([22 U.S.C. 611(b)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/22/611#b)).

(7) Lobbying activities

The term “[lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means[lobbying contacts](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1836374092-1414555997&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)and efforts in support of such contacts, including preparation and planning activities, research and other background work that is intended, at the time it is performed, for use in contacts, and coordination with the [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) of others.

(8) Lobbying contact

(A) DefinitionThe term “[lobbying contact](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1836374092-1414555997&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means any oral or written communication (including an electronic communication) to a [covered executive branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) or a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) that is made on behalf of a[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)with regard to—

(i)

the formulation, modification, or adoption of Federal legislation (including legislative proposals);

(ii)

the formulation, modification, or adoption of a Federal rule, regulation, Executive order, or any other program, policy, or position of the United [States](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) Government;

(iii)

the administration or execution of a Federal program or policy (including the negotiation, award, or administration of a Federal contract, grant, loan, permit, or license); or

(iv)

the nomination or confirmation of a person for a position subject to confirmation by the Senate.

(B) ExceptionsThe term “[lobbying contact](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1836374092-1414555997&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” does not include a communication that is—

(i)

made by a [public official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-56987966-901562783&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) acting in the [public official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-56987966-901562783&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)’s official capacity;

(ii)

made by a representative of a [media organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-684343279-901562779&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) if the purpose of the communication is gathering and disseminating news and information to the public;

(iii)

made in a speech, article, publication or other material that is distributed and made available to the public, or through radio, television, cable television, or other medium of mass communication;

(iv)

made on behalf of a government of a foreign country or a foreign political party and disclosed under the [Foreign Agents Registration Act of 1938](https://www.law.cornell.edu/topn/foreign_agents_registration_act_of_1938) ([22 U.S.C. 611](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/22/611) et seq.);

(v)

a request for a meeting, a request for the status of an action, or any other similar administrative request, if the request does not include an attempt to influence a [covered executive branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) or a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602);

(vi)

made in the course of participation in an advisory committee subject to chapter 10 of title 5;

(vii)

testimony given before a committee, subcommittee, or task force of the Congress, or submitted for inclusion in the public record of a hearing conducted by such committee, subcommittee, or task force;

(viii)

information provided in writing in response to an oral or written request by a [covered executive branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) or a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) for specific information;

(ix)

required by subpoena, civil investigative demand, or otherwise compelled by statute, regulation, or other action of the Congress or an[agency,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) including any communication compelled by a Federal contract, grant, loan, permit, or license;

(x)

made in response to a notice in the Federal Register, Commerce Business Daily, or other similar publication soliciting communications from the public and directed to the [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) official specifically designated in the notice to receive such communications;

(xi)

not possible to report without disclosing information, the unauthorized disclosure of which is prohibited by law;

(xii) made to an official in an [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) with regard to—

(I)

a judicial proceeding or a criminal or civil law enforcement inquiry, investigation, or proceeding; or

(II)

a filing or proceeding that the Government is specifically required by statute or regulation to maintain or conduct on a confidential basis,

if that [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) is charged with responsibility for such proceeding, inquiry, investigation, or filing;

(xiii)

made in compliance with written [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) procedures regarding an adjudication conducted by the [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) under [section 554 of title 5](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/5/554) or substantially similar provisions;

(xiv)

a written comment filed in the course of a public proceeding or any other communication that is made on the record in a public proceeding;

(xv)

a petition for [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) action made in writing and required to be a matter of public record pursuant to established [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) procedures;

(xvi) made on behalf of an individual with regard to that individual’s benefits, employment, or other personal matters involving only that individual, except that this clause does not apply to any communication with—

(I)

a [covered executive branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1052080537-1414555992&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), or

(II)

a [covered legislative branch official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-360285296-1414555993&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) (other than the individual’s elected Members of Congress or[employees](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)who work under such Members’ direct supervision),

with respect to the formulation, modification, or adoption of private legislation for the relief of that individual;

(xvii)

a disclosure by an individual that is protected under the amendments made by the [Whistleblower Protection Act of 1989](https://www.law.cornell.edu/topn/whistleblower_protection_act_of_1989), under chapter 4 of title 5, or under another provision of law;

(xviii) made by—

(I)

a church, its integrated auxiliary, or a convention or association of churches that is exempt from filing a Federal income tax return under paragraph 2(A)(i) of [section 6033(a) of title 26](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/26/6033#a), or

(II)

a religious order that is exempt from filing a Federal income tax return under paragraph (2)(A)(iii) of such section 6033(a); and

(xix) between—

(I)

officials of a self-regulatory [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) (as defined in section 3(a)(26) of the Securities Exchange Act [[15 U.S.C. 78c(a)(26)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/78c#a_26)]) that is registered with or established by the Securities and Exchange Commission as required by that Act [[15 U.S.C. 78a](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/78a) et seq.] or a similar[organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)that is designated by or registered with the Commodities Future Trading Commission as provided under the [Commodity Exchange Act](https://www.law.cornell.edu/topn/capper-tincher_act_grain_futures) [[7 U.S.C. 1](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/7/1) et seq.]; and

(II)

the Securities and Exchange Commission or the Commodities Future Trading Commission, respectively;

relating to the regulatory responsibilities of such [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) under that Act.

(9) Lobbying firm

The term “[lobbying firm](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1790013038-1414555998&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means a [person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) that has 1 or more[employees](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)who are[lobbyists](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)on behalf of a[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)other than that [person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602). The term also includes a self-employed individual who is a[lobbyist.](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)

(10) Lobbyist

The term “[lobbyist](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1873487092-901562778&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means any individual who is employed or retained by a[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)for financial or other compensation for services that include more than one [lobbying contact](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1836374092-1414555997&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), other than an individual whose [lobbying activities](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-35981377-1414555996&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) constitute less than 20 percent of the time engaged in the services provided by such individual to that[client](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1357712437-1414555991&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)over a 3-month period.

(11) Media organization

The term “[media organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-684343279-901562779&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means a[person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)engaged in disseminating information to the general public through a newspaper, magazine, other publication, radio, television, cable television, or other medium of mass communication.

(12) Member of Congress

The term “Member of Congress” means a Senator or a Representative in, or Delegate or Resident Commissioner to, the Congress.

(13) Organization

The term “[organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means a [person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) other than an individual.

(14) Person or entity

The term “[person or entity](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1848458827-901562782&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means any individual, corporation, company, foundation, association, labor[organization,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) firm, partnership, society, joint stock company, group of[organizations,](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) or[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)or local government.

(15) Public officialThe term “[public official](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-56987966-901562783&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means any elected official, appointed official, or[employee](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1193469614-1414555994&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)of—

(A) a Federal, [State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), or local unit of government in the United [States](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) other than—

(i)

a college or university;

(ii)

a government-sponsored enterprise (as defined in [section 622(8) of this title](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/622#8));

(iii)

a public utility that provides gas, electricity, water, or communications;

(iv)

a guaranty [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) (as defined in [section 1085(j) of title 20](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/20/1085#j)), including any affiliate of such an[agency;](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) or

(v)

an [agency](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1419699195-1414555990&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) of any[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)functioning as a student loan secondary market pursuant to [section 1085(d)(1)(F) of title 20](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/20/1085#d_1_F);

(B)

a Government corporation (as defined in [section 9101 of title 31](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/31/9101));

(C)

an [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) of[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)or local elected or appointed officials other than officials of an entity described in clause (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), or (v) of subparagraph (A);

(D)

an Indian tribe (as defined in [section 5304(e) of title 25](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/25/5304#e); [[1]](#fn002066)

(E)

a national or [State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602) political party or any organizational unit thereof; or

(F)

a national, regional, or local unit of any foreign government, or a group of governments acting together as an international [organization](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-1178922291-901562781&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602).

(16) State

The term “[State](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602)” means each of the several [States](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602), the District of Columbia, and any commonwealth, territory, or possession of the United [States](https://www.law.cornell.edu/definitions/uscode.php?width=840&height=800&iframe=true&def_id=2-USC-80204913-901562784&term_occur=999&term_src=title:2:chapter:26:section:1602).

L. 105–166](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/Pub._L._105-166), §§ 2, 3, Apr. 6, 1998, [112 Stat. 38](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rio/citation/112_Stat._38); [Pub. content.text: Now the reconciliation memo:

input.content:
# The "Mitch McConnell Building" fund — outside record vs. federal disclosure

**Question:** Get the complete list of everyone who funded the Republican Party of Kentucky building
fund (the "Mitch McConnell Building") from the real outside record, line it up against who disclosed
that gift on their own federal lobbying forms, show exactly who reported and who didn't — and where a
donor apparently should have disclosed federally and didn't, check whether the law actually required it.

**Bottom line:** Six corporations put **$1,650,000** into the fund in the last quarter of 2022. Three of
them (**Pfizer, AT&T, Delta**) also disclosed the gift on their federal LD-203 lobbying reports. Three of
them (**MetLife, Altria, Comcast — $500,000 combined**) did **not**, even though all three are active
federal lobbying registrants who file those very reports. Whether they were legally required to disclose
turns on one unsettled reading of the statute — so this is a *question for a lawyer and for the companies*,
not a finding of a violation.

---

## The complete outside record (Kentucky Registry of Election Finance)

Unlike the self-reported federal forms, the authoritative record of who funded the building is the state
campaign-finance filing the **Republican Party of Kentucky Building Fund** itself submitted to the
**Kentucky Registry of Election Finance**. As reported by the Kentucky Lantern (Jan 9, 2023), that filing
listed the fund's entire Q4-2022 haul — six corporate donors, $1.65M, into an account that had raised only
$6,000 in the first three quarters of the year:

| Donor | Amount (state record) |
|---|---|
| Pfizer Inc. | $1,000,000 |
| Metropolitan Life Insurance Co. (MetLife) | $300,000 |
| Altria Client Services LLC | $100,000 |
| Comcast Corp. | $100,000 |
| AT&T | $100,000 |
| Delta Air Lines | $50,000 |
| **Total** | **$1,650,000** |

Context that matters for the legal question below: Kentucky bars corporate contributions to a party's
regular committee, but a **2017 state law** created these party "building funds" that *can* take
**unlimited corporate money**, restricted to buying/renovating the headquarters. The headquarters is
signed as the **"Mitch McConnell Building."** So this is corporate money, legal under Kentucky law, flowing
to a building named for the sitting Senate Republican leader — money that could never go to the party
directly.

## Lined up against the federal lobbying record (LD-203)

I searched every federal LD-203 contribution item filed by these companies across 2022–2026 Q1 for any
payment tied to the building fund or McConnell. Here is the match-up for the Q4-2022 cohort:

| Donor | State record | Disclosed on federal LD-203 (honorary)? | Federal detail |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pfizer** | $1,000,000 | **YES** | $1,000,000, 2022-12-27, honoree "Mitch McConnell" (uuid `7fcdd899`) |
| **AT&T** | $100,000 | **YES** | $100,000, 2022-12-15, payee "RPK Building Fund," honoree McConnell (uuid `19b89a8f`) |
| **Delta** | $50,000 | **YES** | disclosed — but appears **twice** ($50k ×2, 2022-11-29), likely an original+amendment duplicate |
| **MetLife** | $300,000 | **NO** | MetLife Group Inc. filed 564 LD-203 items 2022–25; **zero** building-fund/McConnell items |
| **Altria** | $100,000 | **NO** | Altria filed 1,602 LD-203 items; its only KY items are FECA PAC gifts to McConnell's leadership PAC, not the building fund |
| **Comcast** | $100,000 | **NO** | Comcast filed 3,186 LD-203 items; its KY items are ordinary FECA party gifts, not the $100k building gift |

**Disclosed federally: $1,150,000 (Pfizer + AT&T + Delta). Absent from the federal honorary record:
$500,000 (MetLife + Altria + Comcast).**

Two things make the gap notable rather than trivial. First, the non-disclosers aren't small players who
never touch these forms — **MetLife, Altria, and Comcast are among the most prolific LD-203 filers in the
dataset**; they simply have no line for this gift. Second, two of their peers (**Pfizer and AT&T**) looked
at the *identical* gift to the *same* fund and did report it, tagging McConnell as the honoree — so the
industry's own behavior suggests the gift was understood to be reportable.

*(Note: the federal record also shows continued building-fund giving in later years — AT&T $100k in 2023
and 2024, Microsoft $100k in 2023 and $25k in 2025 — which I can't reconcile against the state side without
the fund's 2023–2025 KREF reports. The clean, fully-reconciled cohort is Q4 2022.)*

## Did the law require MetLife, Altria, and Comcast to disclose it? (statute, actually read)

**Verdict: genuinely unclear — leaning "disclosure appears to have been required" — but this is a question
for a lawyer, not a violation I can assert.** I read the operative text.

**Governing provision:** 2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E) (Honest Leadership and Open Government Act of 2007), read
at law.cornell.edu as-of 2026-07-15; definitions at § 1602(4), (12).

Walking the elements against these three donors:

1. **Is the donor a covered filer?** The duty falls on anyone "registered or required to register" under
   the LDA and their listed lobbyists. **Met** — MetLife Group Inc., Altria Client Services LLC, and Comcast
   Corporation are all active federal registrants filing LD-203s in this exact period.
2. **Were funds contributed in the period?** **Met** — $300k / $100k / $100k in the second half of 2022.
3. **To an entity "named for" a covered official, or given "in recognition of" such official**
   (§ 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii))? The official prong is **met** — McConnell is a "Member of Congress," squarely a
   "covered legislative branch official" (§ 1602(4)(A), (12)). The **"named for / in recognition of" prong
   is the crux**: the *building* is the "Mitch McConnell Building," but the *recipient entity* is the "RPK
   Building Fund," which is not itself named for him. A donor could argue (E) reaches only a gift to an
   entity literally named for the official; the counter-argument — the one Pfizer and AT&T apparently
   acted on — is that funding a building named for and honoring McConnell is a payment "in recognition of"
   him. Reasonable filers could genuinely differ here.
4. **Does the FEC exception apply?** No. Subparagraph (E) exempts funds "provided to a person who is
   required to report the receipt of the funds under [52 U.S.C. § 30104]" — i.e., an FEC filer. The building
   fund reports to the **Kentucky** Registry, not the FEC; indeed Kentucky's 2017 law lets it take unlimited
   corporate money precisely because it sits *outside* the federal system. So the carve-out does not exempt
   the gift. (By contrast, the small FECA party gifts these same companies *did* report to the FEC correctly
   stay out of the honorary bucket — the system worked there.)

**So:** all three are covered filers; the money moved; the recipient is not an FEC filer; and the building
bears a sitting senator's name. If § 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii) reaches a gift to a fund for a building named for a
senator — as Pfizer and AT&T evidently concluded — then MetLife, Altria, and Comcast had the same duty and
did not file. If the provision reaches only gifts to an entity *literally* named for the official, then none
of them were required (and Pfizer/AT&T arguably over-disclosed). That single interpretive question decides it.

**What would change the answer:** (a) proof the check-writing entity wasn't the registrant or acting for it
— most live for **MetLife**, where the state record names "Metropolitan Life Insurance Co." while the federal
registrant is "MetLife Group, Inc." (possibly different corporate entities); (b) an official Secretary-of-the-
Senate / House Clerk guidance construing (E) narrowly or broadly; (c) the donor having reported it on a filing
outside this dataset.

**For a lawyer to confirm:** does § 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii)'s "named for … or in recognition of" reach a corporate
gift to a state-party building fund whose headquarters is named for a sitting senator — and is that fund
definitively not an FEC registrant?

## Before any of this is used

No party has been contacted. A reporter should seek comment from MetLife, Altria, and Comcast (why the
building-fund gift isn't on their LD-203s), from Pfizer and AT&T (how they concluded it was reportable), and
from the Republican Party of Kentucky and Sen. McConnell's office — and a lawyer should review the § 1604(d)
reading before publishing any "should have disclosed" framing.

## Sources & provenance

- Complete outside donor list (KREF Q4-2022 filing): Kentucky Lantern, "Pfizer gives $1 million to
  Republican Party of Kentucky to expand its headquarters" (Jan 9, 2023) —
  https://kentuckylantern.com/2023/01/09/pfizer-gives-1-million-to-republican-party-of-kentucky-to-expand-its-headquarters/
- Federal reconciliation: `data/senate/*/contributions/contributions_*.json`, all filers, all types —
  reproduced by `investigation_honorary/work/reconcile.py`; row-level table in
  `investigation_honorary/work/reconciliation_table.csv`. Key uuids: Pfizer `7fcdd899-5c8c-46cd-94c3-9fa9eb8993b5`,
  AT&T `19b89a8f-3cf2-4d2b-9d8b-5cff6e59163d`, Delta `e32ab220…` / `7c2ee09b…`.
- Statute: 2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E) and § 1602(4),(12), https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1604 and
  https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1602 (as-of 2026-07-15).

# The "Mitch McConnell Building" fund — outside record vs. federal disclosure

**Question:** Get the complete list of everyone who funded the Republican Party of Kentucky building
fund (the "Mitch McConnell Building") from the real outside record, line it up against who disclosed
that gift on their own federal lobbying forms, show exactly who reported and who didn't — and where a
donor apparently should have disclosed federally and didn't, check whether the law actually required it.

**Bottom line:** Six corporations put **$1,650,000** into the fund in the last quarter of 2022. Three of
them (**Pfizer, AT&T, Delta**) also disclosed the gift on their federal LD-203 lobbying reports. Three of
them (**MetLife, Altria, Comcast — $500,000 combined**) did **not**, even though all three are active
federal lobbying registrants who file those very reports. Whether they were legally required to disclose
turns on one unsettled reading of the statute — so this is a *question for a lawyer and for the companies*,
not a finding of a violation.

Unlike the self-reported federal forms, the authoritative record of who funded the building is the state
campaign-finance filing the **Republican Party of Kentucky Building Fund** itself submitted to the
**Kentucky Registry of Election Finance**. As reported by the Kentucky Lantern (Jan 9, 2023), that filing
listed the fund's entire Q4-2022 haul — six corporate donors, $1.65M, into an account that had raised only
$6,000 in the first three quarters of the year:

| Donor | Amount (state record) |
|---|---|
| Pfizer Inc. | $1,000,000 |
| Metropolitan Life Insurance Co. (MetLife) | $300,000 |
| Altria Client Services LLC | $100,000 |
| Comcast Corp. | $100,000 |
| AT&T | $100,000 |
| Delta Air Lines | $50,000 |
| **Total** | **$1,650,000** |

Context that matters for the legal question below: Kentucky bars corporate contributions to a party's
regular committee, but a **2017 state law** created these party "building funds" that *can* take
**unlimited corporate money**, restricted to buying/renovating the headquarters. The headquarters is
signed as the **"Mitch McConnell Building."** So this is corporate money, legal under Kentucky law, flowing
to a building named for the sitting Senate Republican leader — money that could never go to the party
directly.

## Lined up against the federal lobbying record (LD-203)

I searched every federal LD-203 contribution item filed by these companies across 2022–2026 Q1 for any

| **Altria** | $100,000 | **NO** | Altria filed 1,602 LD-203 items; its only KY items are FECA PAC gifts to McConnell's leadership PAC, not the building fund |
| **Comcast** | $100,000 | **NO** | Comcast filed 3,186 LD-203 items; its KY items are ordinary FECA party gifts, not the $100k building gift |

---
id: T-0007
title: "McConnell-building fund: $500k of corporate money in KY state record but absent from federal LD-203"
status: chasing
priority: 1
workflow_step: "KREF outside-record (via Ky Lantern) vs full federal LD-203 reconcile.py; checking-the-law on 2 USC 1604(d)(1)(E) + 1602 defs"
entities: [rpk-mcconnell-building, honorary-giving-channel, metlife, altria, comcast]
findings: [F-008]
source_records:
  - "https://kentuckylantern.com/2023/01/09/pfizer-gives-1-million-to-republican-party-of-kentucky-to-expand-its-headquarters/"
  - "data/senate/2022/contributions/contributions_2022.json (all filers; reconcile.py)"
  - "2 U.S.C. 1604(d)(1)(E), 1602(4)/(12) (read law.cornell.edu, as-of 2026-07-15)"
next_action: >
  Reporting-out + one open legal question. Seek comment from MetLife, Altria, Comcast (why the building-
  fund gift is not on their LD-203s), Pfizer + AT&T (how they concluded it WAS reportable), RPK + McConnell
  office. Resolve the entity-identity question for MetLife (KREF 'Metropolitan Life Insurance Co.' vs
  federal registrant 'MetLife Group, Inc.'). Put the 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii) "named for/in recognition of"
  reading to an election-law lawyer. Optional: pull the fund's 2023-2025 KREF reports to reconcile the
  later AT&T/Microsoft federal building items against the state side.
---

## Hypothesis (documented; one open legal question)
The complete outside record of who funded the "Mitch McConnell Building" (Republican Party of Kentucky
Building Fund) diverges from the federal lobbying-disclosure record: half a million dollars visible in
Kentucky's state filings never appears in the donors' own federal LD-203 honorary reports.

## What's documented (F-008)
- KREF Q4-2022 building-fund report: 6 corporate donors, $1.65M — Pfizer $1M, MetLife $300k, Altria $100k,
  Comcast $100k, AT&T $100k, Delta $50k (source: Ky Lantern citing the KREF filing).
- Federal LD-203 (honorary/he) reconciliation:
  DISCLOSED: Pfizer $1M (uuid 7fcdd899), AT&T $100k (uuid 19b89a8f), Delta $50k (shown twice fed = likely
  original+amendment) => $1,150,000 disclosed.
  NOT disclosed: MetLife $300k, Altria $100k, Comcast $100k => $500,000 absent from federal honorary record.
- The three non-disclosers ARE active federal registrants (MetLife Group 564 LD-203 items, Altria 1,602,
  Comcast 3,186); their only KY federal items are ordinary FECA PAC gifts, not the building-fund checks.

## Legal question (checking-the-law; genuinely-unclear, leans "was required")
2 U.S.C. 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii) requires a registrant to disclose funds given to an entity "named for" a covered
official or "in recognition of" such official; FEC-reportable gifts are carved out. Elements: (1) covered
filer = MET (all three registrants); (2) funds disbursed = MET; (3) covered official = MET (McConnell is a
Member of Congress, 1602(4)(A)/(12)); the "named for/in recognition of" prong is the crux — the building is
named for McConnell but the recipient entity is the "RPK Building Fund." (4) FEC exception does NOT apply —
the fund reports to the KY Registry, not the FEC. Pfizer + AT&T treated the identical gift as reportable and
filed it, which cuts toward "covered." Verdict: appears required, but a fair textual argument runs the other
way => question for a lawyer; assert only "possible." Would change: MetLife entity-identity mismatch;
official LDA guidance on (E); a filing outside this dataset.

## Framing rules
Do NOT assert a disclosure violation. Strongest statement: "$500k appears in the state record but not in the
federal honorary record; whether the law required these registrants to disclose it turns on an unsettled
reading of 1604(d)(1)(E) and is a question for a lawyer." Seek comment from all parties first.

content.text:
I chased it all the way down. The real outside record is the **Republican Party of Kentucky Building Fund's own filing with the Kentucky Registry of Election Finance** — the state account that, under a 2017 Kentucky law, can take unlimited corporate money for the headquarters signed as the "Mitch McConnell Building."

**The complete Q4-2022 donor list (state record): six companies, $1.65M** — Pfizer $1M, MetLife $300k, Altria $100k, Comcast $100k, AT&T $100k, Delta $50k.

**Lined up against their own federal LD-203 lobbying reports:**

- **Reported it federally ($1.15M):** Pfizer, AT&T, Delta.
- **Did NOT ($500k):** MetLife $300k, Altria $100k, Comcast $100k — and this is the part that stands out: all three are among the heaviest LD-203 filers in the whole dataset (MetLife Group 564 items, Altria 1,602, Comcast 3,186). They file these reports constantly; they just have no line for this gift. Their only Kentucky federal entries are ordinary FECA PAC contributions, not the building-fund checks.

**Did the law require them to disclose it?** I read the actual statute (2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E), definitions at § 1602). Three of the four elements clearly point to "yes": all three are covered registrants, the money moved, McConnell is a covered official, and the FEC carve-out doesn't apply (the fund reports to Kentucky, not the FEC). The whole thing hinges on one unsettled phrase — whether a gift to a *fund* for a *building* named for a senator counts as a payment "named for … or in recognition of" that official. **Pfizer and AT&T looked at the identical gift and filed it, which cuts toward "yes"** — but a company could argue the recipient entity isn't literally named for McConnell. So the honest verdict is *appears required, genuinely unclear, a question for a lawyer* — not a violation I can assert.

One caveat worth flagging before anyone runs with MetLife: the state record names "Metropolitan Life Insurance Co." while the federal registrant is "MetLife Group, Inc." — possibly different corporate entities, which could dissolve the duty for that one. That's the first fact to nail down.

I updated the case file — thread T-0007 is now the open/chasing lead (finding F-008 with the legal flag), lint clean.

Sources: [Kentucky Lantern — the KREF building-fund filing](https://kentuckylantern.com/2023/01/09/pfizer-gives-1-million-to-republican-party-of-kentucky-to-expand-its-headquarters/); [2 U.S.C. § 1604](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1604); [2 U.S.C. § 1602](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1602)

input.command:
cd /sessions/wizardly-fervent-mayer/mnt/CorpusData && python3 -c "
import json,glob,re
def norm(s): return re.sub(r'[^a-z]',' ',(s or '').lower())
rows=[]
for path in sorted(glob.glob('data/senate/*/contributions/contributions_*.json')):
    for rec in json.load(open(path)):
        reg=rec.get('registrant') or {}; regname=reg.get('name') if isinstance(reg,dict) else reg
        for it in (rec.get('contribution_items') or []):
            p=norm(it.get('payee_name')); h=norm(it.get('honoree_name'))
            # building-fund specific (not general RPK federal account / PAC)
            is_bldg = ('building' in p and 'kentucky' in p) or ('mcconnell building' in p) or ('mcconnell building' in h)
            if it.get('contribution_type')=='he' and (('kentucky' in p and 'republican' in p) or 'mcconnell building' in p or ('mcconnell' in h and 'kentucky' in p)):
                rows.append((regname, it.get('filing_year') if False else rec.get('filing_year'), it.get('date'), float(it.get('amount') or 0), it.get('payee_name'), it.get('honoree_name'), rec.get('filing_uuid')))
print('=== ALL federal LD-203 honorary (he) items to RPK/McConnell building, ALL YEARS ===')
tot=0
for r in sorted(rows,key=lambda x:(x[2] or '')):
    tot+=r[3]
    print(f\"  {r[2]} | {r[0][:34]:34} | \${r[3]:>10,.0f} | payee={r[4][:36]:36} | hon={(r[5] or '')[:22]:22} | {r[6][:8]}\")
print(f'  TOTAL federal he building items: \${tot:,.0f} ({len(rows)} items)')
from collections import defaultdict
byf=defaultdict(float)
for r in rows: byf[r[0]]+=r[3]
print('  by filer:', {k:round(v) for k,v in sorted(byf.items(),key=lambda x:-x[1])})
"

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Republican Party of Kentucky building fund, corporate donors, and McConnell:

## Project Overview

The cost to expand the headquarters of the Republican Party of Kentucky rose above $4.4 million in the final quarter of 2025 as the project was completed. The RPK headquarters is named the "Mitch McConnell Building" in honor of Kentucky's senior senator.

## Corporate Donors

The effort was nearly totally funded by massive contributions from a mere 21 donors, nearly all of them corporations or other entities with significant lobbying presences in Washington and/or Frankfort.

Some of the major corporate donors include:

- Pharmaceutical giant Pfizer Inc., of New York, made the largest contribution for the project — $1 million
- Telecommunications companies AT&T and Verizon have each given $300,000 as has Metropolitan Life Insurance Company
- Two big contributions in the summer of 2025 from special interests — $100,000 from Beam Suntory and $75,000 from Boeing
- $150,000 from tobacco giant Altria Client Services, of Richmond, Virginia, and $50,000 from The Jockey Club, of New York City

## Legal Framework

A 2017 law passed by the General Assembly allowed parties to create building funds that could accept contributions from corporations or people in unlimited amounts. Before then, corporations were not allowed to contribute to a state political party, and people were restricted to giving no more than $15,000 a year.

KY Republican HQ upgrade tops $4.4M as special-interest money keeps coming in

by Tom Loftus, Kentucky Lantern
January 7, 2026

FRANKFORT — The cost to expand the headquarters of the Republican Party of Kentucky rose above $4.4 million in the final quarter of 2025 as the project was completed, according to a report filed this week with the Kentucky Registry of Election Finance.

But more large contributions from special interests during the same period allowed the fund to remain easily in balance. The two largest contributions to the fund during the period were $150,000 from tobacco giant Altria Client Services, of Richmond, Virginia,and $50,000 from The Jockey Club, of New York City, according to the new filing by the Republican Party of Kentucky (RPK) Building Fund .

Both Altria and The Jockey Club had made previous contributions to the fund.

Three years ago Kentucky Lantern first reported that the RPK had begun planning and fundraising for the expansion of its headquarters on Third Street in Frankfort and that the pharmaceutical giant Pfizer had donated a stunning $1 million to the effort.

Over the three years a small number of donors — nearly all corporations or other entities with lobbying interests in Washington and/or Frankfort — have made very large contributions totaling more than $4.4 million to pay for the new structure.

The RPK headquarters is named the “Mitch McConnell Building” in honor of Kentucky’s senior senator who is credited with propelling the ascent of the Republican Party to dominance in the state over the last 40 years. McConnell, the longest-serving U.S. Senate leader in history, led Senate Republicans from 2007 until 2025. He announced last year on his 83rd birthday that he would not seek reelection this year, setting off a crowded race to succeed him.

As for the contributions, Adam Hope, spokesman for the RPK, said, “All funding raised for our expansion was done by the book. Contributions to our building fund are a reflection of the strength of our party and the enthusiasm of our supporters, in Kentucky and beyond, who believe in the progress Republicans are making for the betterment of the Commonwealth.”

The project was made possible by a 2017 law passed by the General Assembly in 2017, the first year that Republicans controlled both chambers of the legislature. Before then, corporations were not allowed to contribute to a state political party, and people were restricted to giving no more than $15,000 a year. But the 2017 law allowed parties to create building funds that could accept contributions from corporations or people in unlimited amounts.

The report filed this week by the RPK Building Fund listed $580,000 in expenses during the last three months of 2025. That brought total spending on the project to a bit over $4.4 million.

By far the largest share of spending — about $3.8 million — was paid to contractor Branscum Construction, of Russell Springs. Stengel-Hill Architects, of Louisville, has been paid about $263,000 through 2025, according to the reports. And the third highest expense has been $195,000 paid to Haney Consulting, the political fundraising business run by Laura Haney, a longtime fundraiser for McConnell as well as other Kentucky Republican causes.

The Kentucky Democratic Party reported one major deposit during the last quarter to its building fund of $499,241. This is apparently most of the proceeds from the sale last fall of its old headquarters on Interstate 64. The Kentucky Lantern reported last month that property records filed in Franklin County show that the party sold the old headquarters property for $540,000 and is now headquartered in offices on Ann Street in Frankfort.

Morgan Eaves, executive director of the Kentucky Democratic Party, said, “Our focus has not been on a big new building but on policy – policy that will help Kentuckians who are losing health care and other basic needs because of things like the Republican Big Beautiful Bill.”

Here’s an updated list of donors to the Republican Party of Kentucky Building Fund:

Pfizer Inc., New York, New York, $1,000,000

NWO Resources, Greenwood Village, Colorado, $500,000

Altria Client Services (Philip Morris USA), Richmond, Virginia, $300,000

Metropolitan Life Insurance Co., New York, New York, $300,000

AT&T, St. Louis, Missouri, $300,000

Verizon, Washington, DC, $300,000

Brown-Forman Corp., Louisville, $300,000

The Boeing Company PAC, Arlington, Virginia, $250,000

Beam Suntory, New York, New York, $200,000

Sazerac Inc., Louisville, $150,000

Microsoft Corp., Reno, Nevada, $125,000

Comcast Corp., Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, $100,000

The Jockey Club, New York, New York, $100,000

Churchill Downs, Louisville, $100,000

Keeneland, Lexington, $100,000

Barbara R. Banke Revocable Trust, Geyserville, California, $100,000

Airbnb, Inc., San Francisco, California, $50,000

Delta Air Lines, Atlanta, Georgia, $50,000

National Thoroughbred Racing Assn., Lexington, $50,000

Rescare Inc., Louisville, $12,500

Toyota Motor North America Inc., Plano, Texas, $10,000

Jonathan Goldberg, attorney, Louisville, $10,000

16 smaller contributions, $19,145

KY Republican HQ upgrade tops $4.4M as special-interest money keeps coming in

by Tom Loftus, Kentucky Lantern
January 7, 2026

FRANKFORT — The cost to expand the headquarters of the Republican Party of Kentucky rose above $4.4 million in the final quarter of 2025 as the project was completed, according to a report filed this week with the Kentucky Registry of Election Finance.

But more large contributions from special interests during the same period allowed the fund to remain easily in balance. The two largest contributions to the fund during the period were $150,000 from tobacco giant Altria Client Services, of Richmond, Virginia,and $50,000 from The Jockey Club, of New York City, according to the new filing by the Republican Party of Kentucky (RPK) Building Fund .

Both Altria and The Jockey Club had made previous contributions to the fund.

Three years ago Kentucky Lantern first reported that the RPK had begun planning and fundraising for the expansion of its headquarters on Third Street in Frankfort and that the pharmaceutical giant Pfizer had donated a stunning $1 million to the effort.

Over the three years a small number of donors — nearly all corporations or other entities with lobbying interests in Washington and/or Frankfort — have made very large contributions totaling more than $4.4 million to pay for the new structure.

The RPK headquarters is named the “Mitch McConnell Building” in honor of Kentucky’s senior senator who is credited with propelling the ascent of the Republican Party to dominance in the state over the last 40 years. McConnell, the longest-serving U.S. Senate leader in history, led Senate Republicans from 2007 until 2025. He announced last year on his 83rd birthday that he would not seek reelection this year, setting off a crowded race to succeed him.

As for the contributions, Adam Hope, spokesman for the RPK, said, “All funding raised for our expansion was done by the book. Contributions to our building fund are a reflection of the strength of our party and the enthusiasm of our supporters, in Kentucky and beyond, who believe in the progress Republicans are making for the betterment of the Commonwealth.”

The project was made possible by a 2017 law passed by the General Assembly in 2017, the first year that Republicans controlled both chambers of the legislature. Before then, corporations were not allowed to contribute to a state political party, and people were restricted to giving no more than $15,000 a year. But the 2017 law allowed parties to create building funds that could accept contributions from corporations or people in unlimited amounts.

The report filed this week by the RPK Building Fund listed $580,000 in expenses during the last three months of 2025. That brought total spending on the project to a bit over $4.4 million.

By far the largest share of spending — about $3.8 million — was paid to contractor Branscum Construction, of Russell Springs. Stengel-Hill Architects, of Louisville, has been paid about $263,000 through 2025, according to the reports. And the third highest expense has been $195,000 paid to Haney Consulting, the political fundraising business run by Laura Haney, a longtime fundraiser for McConnell as well as other Kentucky Republican causes.

The Kentucky Democratic Party reported one major deposit during the last quarter to its building fund of $499,241. This is apparently most of the proceeds from the sale last fall of its old headquarters on Interstate 64. The Kentucky Lantern reported last month that property records filed in Franklin County show that the party sold the old headquarters property for $540,000 and is now headquartered in offices on Ann Street in Frankfort.

Morgan Eaves, executive director of the Kentucky Democratic Party, said, “Our focus has not been on a big new building but on policy – policy that will help Kentuckians who are losing health care and other basic needs because of things like the Republican Big Beautiful Bill.”

Here’s an updated list of donors to the Republican Party of Kentucky Building Fund:

Pfizer Inc., New York, New York, $1,000,000

NWO Resources, Greenwood Village, Colorado, $500,000

Altria Client Services (Philip Morris USA), Richmond, Virginia, $300,000

Metropolitan Life Insurance Co., New York, New York, $300,000

AT&T, St. Louis, Missouri, $300,000

Verizon, Washington, DC, $300,000

Brown-Forman Corp., Louisville, $300,000

The Boeing Company PAC, Arlington, Virginia, $250,000

Beam Suntory, New York, New York, $200,000

Sazerac Inc., Louisville, $150,000

Microsoft Corp., Reno, Nevada, $125,000

Comcast Corp., Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, $100,000

The Jockey Club, New York, New York, $100,000

Churchill Downs, Louisville, $100,000

Keeneland, Lexington, $100,000

Barbara R. Banke Revocable Trust, Geyserville, California, $100,000

Airbnb, Inc., San Francisco, California, $50,000

Delta Air Lines, Atlanta, Georgia, $50,000

National Thoroughbred Racing Assn., Lexington, $50,000

Rescare Inc., Louisville, $12,500

Toyota Motor North America Inc., Plano, Texas, $10,000

Jonathan Goldberg, attorney, Louisville, $10,000

16 smaller contributions, $19,145

lastPrompt: I think we're only seeing part of it. Can you look for the later coverage or state filings that show the full set of donors over the whole period, and extend the same reconciliation across all of it,…

content.text: This is the complete picture — the fund reached **$4.4M from ~22 named donors** over three years, and the Jan 2026 filing gives the full cumulative donor list. Critically, several big donors (NWO Resources $500k, Verizon $300k, Brown-Forman $300k, Boeing PAC $250k, Beam Suntory $200k, the racing interests) never appeared in the single quarter I first looked at. Let me now match every donor against whether they file federal LD-203s at all, and whether they disclosed this gift.

cd /sessions/wizardly-fervent-mayer/mnt/CorpusData && cat > investigation_honorary/work/fullrecon.py << 'PY'
import json,glob,re
from collections import defaultdict
def norm(s): return re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]',' ',(s or '').lower())

# Build: set of all LD-203 registrant names (who files federal contribution reports),
# and all he-building items keyed by normalized registrant.
registrant_names=set()
he_building=defaultdict(float)   # normalized filer -> $ of he building-fund items
any_items_by_filer=defaultdict(int)
for path in sorted(glob.glob('data/senate/*/contributions/contributions_*.json')):
    for rec in json.load(open(path)):
        reg=rec.get('registrant') or {}; rn=reg.get('name') if isinstance(reg,dict) else reg
        if rn: registrant_names.add(rn); any_items_by_filer[norm(rn)]+=1
        for it in (rec.get('contribution_items') or []):
            p=norm(it.get('payee_name')); h=norm(it.get('honoree_name'))
            if it.get('contribution_type')=='he' and (('kentucky' in p and 'republican' in p) or 'mcconnell building' in p):
                he_building[norm(rn)]+=float(it.get('amount') or 0)
reg_norm={norm(r):r for r in registrant_names}

# The complete outside donor list (KREF cumulative, Ky Lantern 2026-01-07)
donors=[
 ("Pfizer Inc.",1000000),("NWO Resources",500000),("Altria Client Services",300000),
 ("Metropolitan Life Insurance Co.",300000),("AT&T",300000),("Verizon",300000),
 ("Brown-Forman Corp.",300000),("The Boeing Company PAC",250000),("Beam Suntory",200000),
 ("Sazerac Inc.",150000),("Microsoft Corp.",125000),("Comcast Corp.",100000),
 ("The Jockey Club",100000),("Churchill Downs",100000),("Keeneland",100000),
 ("Barbara R. Banke Revocable Trust",100000),("Airbnb, Inc.",50000),("Delta Air Lines",50000),
 ("National Thoroughbred Racing Assn.",50000),("Rescare Inc.",12500),
 ("Toyota Motor North America Inc.",10000),("Jonathan Goldberg (attorney)",10000),
 ("16 smaller contributions",19145),
]
# helper: does donor appear as a federal LD-203 registrant? (substring match on key token)
def find_registrant(name):
    n=norm(name)
    toks={'pfizer':'pfizer','altria':'altria','metropolitan life':'metlife','metlife':'metlife',
          'at t':'at t','at&t':'at t','verizon':'verizon','brown forman':'brown forman',
          'boeing':'boeing','beam suntory':'suntory','suntory':'suntory','sazerac':'sazerac',
          'microsoft':'microsoft','comcast':'comcast','jockey club':'jockey','churchill downs':'churchill downs',
          'keeneland':'keeneland','airbnb':'airbnb','delta air':'delta air','thoroughbred':'thoroughbred',
          'rescare':'rescare','toyota':'toyota','nwo resources':'nwo resources'}
    key=None
    for k,v in toks.items():
        if k in n: key=v; break
    if not key: return []
    return sorted({reg_norm[rn] for rn in reg_norm if key in rn})

print(f"{'DONOR':38} {'STATE $':>10} {'FED he BLDG $':>13}  FEDERAL LD-203 REGISTRANT MATCH")
print('-'*110)
disc=notdisc_reg=notdisc_nonreg=0
sd=snr=snn=0
for name,amt in donors:
    matches=find_registrant(name)
    he_amt=0.0
    for m in matches: he_amt+=he_building.get(norm(m),0)
    is_reg=len(matches)>0
    status = "DISCLOSED (he)" if he_amt>0 else ("REGISTRANT, no he item" if is_reg else "not a federal LD-203 filer / n.a.")
    print(f"{name[:38]:38} {amt:>10,} {he_amt:>13,.0f}  {status}  {('['+matches[0][:40]+']') if matches else ''}")
    if he_amt>0: disc+=1; sd+=amt
    elif is_reg: notdisc_reg+=1; snr+=amt
    else: notdisc_nonreg+=1; snn+=amt
print('-'*110)
print(f"TOTAL state fund ~${sum(a for _,a in donors):,}")
print(f"DISCLOSED federally (he building item): {disc} donors, ${sd:,} (state amounts)")
print(f"FEDERAL REGISTRANTS that did NOT disclose a building he item: {notdisc_reg} donors, ${snr:,}")
print(f"Not federal LD-203 filers (no federal duty) / individuals/PAileC-only/unknown: {notdisc_nonreg} donors, ${snn:,}")
PY
python3 investigation_honorary/work/fullrecon.py

input.content:
# The "Mitch McConnell Building" fund — full-period reconciliation (2022–2025)

**Question:** Get the complete list of everyone who funded the Republican Party of Kentucky building fund
(the "Mitch McConnell Building") from the real outside record over the *whole* period, line it up against
who disclosed the gift on their own federal lobbying forms, show exactly who reported and who didn't — and
where a donor apparently should have disclosed federally and didn't, check whether the law required it.

**Bottom line (revised — the single quarter badly understated it):** Over three years the fund took in
**~$4.43 million from about 22 named donors** (plus 16 small gifts), almost all corporations with lobbying
interests. Of that, only **four donors — Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft, Delta ($1.475M)** — disclosed the gift on
their federal LD-203 lobbying reports. **Thirteen other donors that are themselves active federal lobbying
registrants — about $2.12M — did not.** A further ~$0.83M came from donors with no federal lobbying footprint
(so no federal duty), including the second-largest donor of all. Whether the 13 registrants were *required*
to disclose turns on one unsettled reading of the statute — so this is a strong *question for a lawyer and
for the companies*, not a finding of a violation. What changed from the one-quarter look: the gap went from
3 companies / $500k to **13 companies / ~$2.1M.**

---

## The complete outside record (Kentucky Registry of Election Finance, cumulative)

The authoritative record is the RPK Building Fund's own filings with the **Kentucky Registry of Election
Finance**, which the Kentucky Lantern tracked across three years (final accounting Jan 7, 2026, when the
$4.4M project was completed). Kentucky's 2017 law lets party "building funds" take **unlimited corporate
money** for headquarters construction — money that could never go to the party directly. The full cumulative
donor list:

| Donor | State record (cumulative) | Federal LD-203 filer? | Disclosed the gift federally? |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Pfizer Inc. | $1,000,000 | yes | **YES** — $1,000,000 |
| NWO Resources | $500,000 | no | n/a (no federal duty) |
| Altria Client Services | $300,000 | yes | NO |
| Metropolitan Life Insurance Co. | $300,000 | yes* | NO |
| AT&T | $300,000 | yes | **YES** — $300,000 |
| Verizon | $300,000 | yes | NO |
| Brown-Forman Corp. | $300,000 | yes | NO |
| The Boeing Company PAC | $250,000 | yes* | NO |
| Beam Suntory | $200,000 | yes | NO |
| Sazerac Inc. | $150,000 | yes | NO |
| Microsoft Corp. | $125,000 | yes | **YES** — $125,000 |
| Comcast Corp. | $100,000 | yes | NO |
| The Jockey Club | $100,000 | no | n/a |
| Churchill Downs | $100,000 | yes | NO |
| Keeneland | $100,000 | no | n/a |
| Barbara R. Banke Revocable Trust | $100,000 | no (individual) | n/a |
| Airbnb, Inc. | $50,000 | yes | NO |
| Delta Air Lines | $50,000 | yes | **YES** — $50k (shown twice fed) |
| National Thoroughbred Racing Assn. | $50,000 | yes | NO |
| Rescare Inc. | $12,500 | yes* | NO |
| Toyota Motor North America Inc. | $10,000 | yes | NO |
| Jonathan Goldberg (attorney) | $10,000 | no (individual) | n/a |
| 16 smaller contributions | $19,145 | no | n/a |
| **Total** | **~$4,426,645** | | |

\* Entity-name nuance: the state donor and the federal registrant aren't an exact name match — MetLife
("Metropolitan Life Insurance Co." vs registrant "MetLife Group, Inc."), Boeing (gift via "The Boeing Company
PAC" vs registrant "Boeing Company"), Rescare (now "BrightSpring Health Services"). These matter for whose
duty it is (see law section).

## The three-way split

- **Disclosed on their federal LD-203 (honorary "he" item): 4 donors, $1,475,000** — Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft,
  Delta. Cross-validation is clean: AT&T's $300k state total equals its $300k federal ($100k ×3, 2022–24);
  Microsoft $125k = $125k; Pfizer $1M = $1M. (Delta shows $50k in the state record but two $50k entries
  federally — a likely original+amendment duplicate.)
- **Federal lobbying registrants that gave but did NOT disclose a building gift: 13 donors, ~$2,122,500** —
  Altria $300k, MetLife $300k, Verizon $300k, Brown-Forman $300k, Boeing (PAC) $250k, Beam Suntory $200k,
  Sazerac $150k, Comcast $100k, Churchill Downs $100k, Airbnb $50k, National Thoroughbred Racing Assn. $50k,
  Rescare/BrightSpring $12.5k, Toyota $10k. Several of these *did* disclose *other* McConnell honoraria on
  their LD-203s (Toyota reported $100k to the JFK Center in his honor; Airbnb reported $1,500 to AEI; Boeing
  reported a Hudson Institute gala) — they simply left the far larger building-fund gift off.
- **Not federal LD-203 filers, so no federal duty: 6 lines, ~$829,145** — including the **second-largest
  donor of the entire project, NWO Resources ($500,000)**, a privately held company with no federal lobbying
  footprint in the data, plus the horse-racing names Jockey Club and Keeneland, an individual's trust, an
  individual attorney, and 16 small gifts.

The headline, restated cleanly: **of the ~$3.6M that came from entities which file federal lobbying reports,
only $1.475M (4 donors) shows up in the federal honorary record; ~$2.12M (13 donors) does not.**

## Did the law require the 13 to disclose it? (statute, actually read)

**Verdict: genuinely unclear — leaning "disclosure appears to have been required" — a question for a lawyer,
not a violation I can assert.** The expansion *sharpens* rather than changes the earlier analysis: with 4
registrants treating the identical gift as reportable and 13 not, the industry itself is split, which is
exactly what makes it a live legal question.

**Governing provision:** 2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E) (HLOGA 2007); definitions § 1602(4),(12). Read at
law.cornell.edu, as-of 2026-07-15. Walking the elements against the 13 registrants:

1. **Covered filer?** Met — all 13 are active federal LDA registrants filing LD-203s in this period.
2. **Funds contributed in the period?** Met — the state record dates and amounts establish this.
3. **To an entity "named for," or given "in recognition of," a covered official** (§ 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii))? The
   official prong is met — McConnell is a "Member of Congress," a "covered legislative branch official"
   (§ 1602(4)(A),(12)). The **"named for / in recognition of" prong is the crux**: the *building* is the
   "Mitch McConnell Building," but the *recipient entity* is the "RPK Building Fund," not literally named for
   him. Pfizer, AT&T, Microsoft, and Delta read this as reportable and filed it (honoree = McConnell); a
   filer could argue (E) reaches only a gift to an entity literally *named* for the official.
4. **FEC exception?** Does not apply — (E) exempts funds given to an FEC filer; this fund reports to the
   **Kentucky** Registry, not the FEC. (Consistently, the small FECA party gifts these same companies made
   *are* FEC-reported and correctly stay out of the honorary bucket.)

So all 13 are covered filers; the money moved; the recipient isn't an FEC filer; and the building bears a
sitting senator's name. If § 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii) reaches a gift to a fund for a building named for a senator —
as four peer companies concluded — the other 13 had the same duty and didn't file. If it reaches only gifts
to an entity literally named for the official, none were required (and the four over-disclosed). That one
interpretive question decides it.

**What would change the answer, donor by donor:** the entity-identity mismatches are the most live off-ramps
— **MetLife** (check writer "Metropolitan Life Insurance Co." vs registrant "MetLife Group, Inc."), **Boeing**
(gift via its PAC — though (E) expressly covers a "political committee established or controlled by" the
registrant, which cuts *toward* a Boeing duty), and **Rescare/BrightSpring**. Also: an official Secretary-of-
the-Senate/House-Clerk interpretation of (E); or a disclosure filed outside this dataset. One timing note: the
most recent tranches (e.g., Altria's Q4-2025 gift) fall in the H2-2025 LD-203 period due Jan 30, 2026 — but
these donors show *zero* building items across *all* years, so earlier tranches went undisclosed regardless.

**For a lawyer to confirm:** does § 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii)'s "named for … or in recognition of" reach a corporate
gift to a state-party building fund whose headquarters is named for a sitting senator; and are these building
funds definitively not FEC registrants?

## Before any of this is used

No party has been contacted. A reporter should seek comment from the 13 registrants that didn't disclose
(why the building gift isn't on their LD-203s), from the four that did (how they concluded it was reportable),
and from the Republican Party of Kentucky and Sen. McConnell's office — and a lawyer should review the
§ 1604(d) reading before publishing any "should have disclosed" framing. RPK's spokesman has said the fund's
fundraising "was done by the book."

## Sources & provenance

- Complete cumulative donor list (KREF filings): Kentucky Lantern, "KY Republican HQ upgrade tops $4.4M as
  special-interest money keeps coming in" (Jan 7, 2026) —
  https://kentuckylantern.com/2026/01/07/ky-republican-hq-upgrade-tops-4-4m-as-special-interest-money-keeps-coming-in/
  ; first report (Q4-2022 detail): https://kentuckylantern.com/2023/01/09/pfizer-gives-1-million-to-republican-party-of-kentucky-to-expand-its-headquarters/
- Federal reconciliation: `data/senate/*/contributions/contributions_*.json`, all filers, all years —
  reproduced by `investigation_honorary/work/fullrecon.py` and `build_full_table.py`; row-level table in
  `investigation_honorary/work/full_reconciliation_table.csv`. Disclosed uuids: Pfizer `7fcdd899…`,
  AT&T `19b89a8f…`/`4bec4d65…`/`66073ffa…`, Microsoft `4419ba3f…`/`2b9a30d6…`, Delta `e32ab220…`/`7c2ee09b…`.
- Statute: 2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E) and § 1602(4),(12) — https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1604 ,
  https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1602 (as-of 2026-07-15).

content.content: The file /Users/mitulkrishna/Documents/BEAM/CorpusData/investigation_honorary/MCCONNELL_BUILDING_reconciliation.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

   *are* FEC-reported and correctly stay out of the honorary bucket.)

So all 13 are covered filers; the money moved; the recipient isn't an FEC filer; and the building bears a
sitting senator's name. If § 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii) reaches a gift to a fund for a building named for a senator —
as four peer companies concluded — the other 13 had the same duty and didn't file. If it reaches only gifts
to an entity literally named for the official, none were required (and the four over-disclosed). That one
interpretive question decides it.

**What would change the answer, donor by donor:** the entity-identity mismatches are the most live off-ramps
— **MetLife** (check writer "Metropolitan Life Insurance Co." vs registrant "MetLife Group, Inc."), **Boeing**
(gift via its PAC — though (E) expressly covers a "political committee established or controlled by" the
registrant, which cuts *toward* a Boeing duty), and **Rescare/BrightSpring**. Also: an official Secretary-of-
the-Senate/House-Clerk interpretation of (E); or a disclosure filed outside this dataset. One timing note: the
most recent tranches (e.g., Altria's Q4-2025 gift) fall in the H2-2025 LD-203 period due Jan 30, 2026 — but
these donors show *zero* building items across *all* years, so earlier tranches went undisclosed regardless.

**For a lawyer to confirm:** does § 1604(d)(1)(E)(ii)'s "named for … or in recognition of" reach a corporate
gift to a state-party building fund whose headquarters is named for a sitting senator; and are these building
funds definitively not FEC registrants?

## Before any of this is used

No party has been contacted. A reporter should seek comment from the 13 registrants that didn't disclose
(why the building gift isn't on their LD-203s), from the four that did (how they concluded it was reportable),
and from the Republican Party of Kentucky and Sen. McConnell's office — and a lawyer should review the
§ 1604(d) reading before publishing any "should have disclosed" framing. RPK's spokesman has said the fund's
fundraising "was done by the book."

## Sources & provenance

- Complete cumulative donor list (KREF filings): Kentucky Lantern, "KY Republican HQ upgrade tops $4.4M as
  special-interest money keeps coming in" (Jan 7, 2026) —
  https://kentuckylantern.com/2026/01/07/ky-republican-hq-upgrade-tops-4-4m-as-special-interest-money-keeps-coming-in/
  ; first report (Q4-2022 detail): https://kentuckylantern.com/2023/01/09/pfizer-gives-1-million-to-republican-party-of-kentucky-to-expand-its-headquarters/
- Federal reconciliation: `data/senate/*/contributions/contributions_*.json`, all filers, all years —
  reproduced by `investigation_honorary/work/fullrecon.py` and `build_full_table.py`; row-level table in
  `investigation_honorary/work/full_reconciliation_table.csv`. Disclosed uuids: Pfizer `7fcdd899…`,
  AT&T `19b89a8f…`/`4bec4d65…`/`66073ffa…`, Microsoft `4419ba3f…`/`2b9a30d6…`, Delta `e32ab220…`/`7c2ee09b…`.
- Statute: 2 U.S.C. § 1604(d)(1)(E) and § 1602(4),(12) — https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1604 ,
  https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1602 (as-of 2026-07-15).

+   (§ 1602(4)(A),(12)). The **"named for / in recognition of" prong is the crux**: the *building* is the

+So all 13 are covered filers; the money moved; the recipient isn't an FEC filer; and the building bears a

+to an entity literally named for the official, none were required (and the four over-disclosed). That one
